Icons doc ok
./src/icons/Icons.mdx
Curated catalogue of all 32 Breeze icon exports, with sizing and accessibility guidance.
import { Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './index.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Curated catalogue of all 32 Breeze icon exports, with sizing and accessibility guidance."
/>

# Icons

Breeze exports a curated set of 32 interface icons through its dedicated `/icons` subpath. Each wrapper has a stable Breeze name, inherits the surrounding text colour, and is decorative by default.

## Import

```tsx
import {
  AddIcon,
  CheckIcon,
  SearchIcon,
  type IconProps,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

Do not import icon implementations from `lucide-react`, Breeze source paths, or the package root. Import only the named icons a module uses.

## When to use

- Choose the icon whose documented meaning most closely matches the interface concept.
- Compose decorative icons beside visible labels in Breeze actions, links, fields, and markers.
- Add `aria-label` only when the standalone glyph itself provides information not expressed in nearby text.

## When not to use

- Use `Logo` for the Motech Development brand mark.
- Use `Avatar` for a person or entity identity.
- Use text instead when no curated icon clearly communicates the concept.
- Do not use icons as decoration without functional or information value, or rely on a glyph as the only cue for a complex or unfamiliar concept.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Button } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { AddIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function AddItemButton() {
  return (
    <Button>
      <AddIcon />
      Add item
    </Button>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Catalogue} />

## Catalogue

| Export             | Intended meaning                                                 |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `AddIcon`          | Create or add an item.                                           |
| `ArrowLeftIcon`    | Leftward movement or navigation.                                 |
| `ArrowRightIcon`   | Rightward movement or navigation.                                |
| `BuildingIcon`     | Organisation, workplace, or company details.                     |
| `CalendarIcon`     | Date, schedule, or calendar surface.                             |
| `ChartIcon`        | Summary, dashboard, trend, or analytics.                         |
| `CheckIcon`        | Completed, confirmed, or selected state.                         |
| `ChevronDownIcon`  | Downward disclosure or movement.                                 |
| `ChevronLeftIcon`  | Leftward disclosure or compact navigation.                       |
| `ChevronRightIcon` | Rightward disclosure or compact navigation.                      |
| `ChevronUpIcon`    | Upward disclosure or movement.                                   |
| `CloseIcon`        | Close, dismiss, or clear.                                        |
| `DeleteIcon`       | Destructive removal.                                             |
| `DownloadIcon`     | Download or obtain a resource.                                   |
| `EditIcon`         | Edit or modify content.                                          |
| `ExternalLinkIcon` | Destination outside the current application or browsing context. |
| `EyeIcon`          | Show, reveal, or visible state.                                  |
| `EyeOffIcon`       | Hide, conceal, or hidden state.                                  |
| `FileTextIcon`     | Document, report, or written record.                             |
| `HelpIcon`         | Contextual help or guidance.                                     |
| `InfoIcon`         | Neutral information.                                             |
| `MenuIcon`         | Open compact navigation or a menu surface.                       |
| `MoreIcon`         | Additional actions or options.                                   |
| `PaperclipIcon`    | File attachment.                                                 |
| `SearchIcon`       | Search or query.                                                 |
| `SettingsIcon`     | Settings or configuration.                                       |
| `SignOutIcon`      | End an authenticated session.                                    |
| `UploadIcon`       | Upload or supply a resource.                                     |
| `UserIcon`         | Person, account, or individual identity.                         |
| `UsersIcon`        | Group, team, or collection of people.                            |
| `WalletIcon`       | Money, wallet, or transaction context.                           |
| `WarningIcon`      | Caution, risk, or warning state.                                 |

Names express the stable public contract; the underlying artwork may evolve. Directional meanings are literal. In a right-to-left interface, choose the arrow or chevron matching the intended physical or navigation direction rather than assuming automatic mirroring.

## Decorative and meaningful icons

Without an `aria-label`, every icon renders with `aria-hidden="true"` and `focusable="false"`. This is correct beside visible text:

```tsx
import { Link } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { ExternalLinkIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function ExternalLink() {
  return (
    <Link href="https://example.com" rel="noreferrer" target="_blank">
      External guidance <ExternalLinkIcon />
    </Link>
  );
}
```

When the icon itself is meaningful, `aria-label` removes `aria-hidden` and adds `role="img"`. The label must name the information conveyed, not describe the artwork:

```tsx
import { InfoIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function InformationStatus() {
  return <InfoIcon aria-label="Information" size={24} />;
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.MeaningfulIcon} />

For icon-only actions, prefer the action component's accessible-label API or visually hidden action text so the control is named as a verb. “Delete item” is a useful button name; “trash icon” is not. Icons never enter the tab order themselves.

## Sizing and colour

`size` defaults to `1em` and accepts a CSS-pixel number or CSS length such as `'1.25rem'`. Both rendered width and height use that value. The default aligns naturally with surrounding text and responds to a parent's text size, including inside `IconTile`.

Icons use `currentColor`, so they inherit the text colour of the containing Breeze component. Use semantic component variants for colour whenever possible. All icons use Lucide strokes, including the warning triangle rendered by `WarningIcon`. `strokeWidth` defaults to `2` and adjusts stroke detail, but should normally retain the canonical value.

```tsx
import { Inline } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { CheckIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function IconSizes() {
  return (
    <Inline align="center" gap="md" wrap={false}>
      <CheckIcon />
      <CheckIcon size={20} />
      <CheckIcon size="1.5rem" />
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

## API

| Prop          | Type               | Default        | Description                                                                    |
| ------------- | ------------------ | -------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `size`        | `number \| string` | `'1em'`        | CSS-pixel number or CSS length used for both width and height.                 |
| `strokeWidth` | `number`           | `2`            | Stroke thickness for line artwork and warning detail.                          |
| `aria-label`  | `string`           | —              | Makes the icon a named semantic image; omission makes it decorative.           |
| `className`   | `string`           | —              | Placement and composition classes.                                             |
| `color`       | `string`           | `currentColor` | Relevant native SVG colour attribute; inheriting from the parent is preferred. |
| `ref`         | not exposed        | —              | Curated icons do not currently expose a ref prop.                              |

Other relevant native SVG attributes, including `aria-describedby`, `data-*`, `id`, and presentation attributes, are accepted. `children`, `height`, and `width` are excluded. A supplied `style` may provide relevant SVG presentation properties, but `size` remains the supported width and height API and semantic component variants remain the supported colour route.

## State, internationalisation, and limitations

Icons have no controlled, uncontrolled, disabled, loading, invalid, empty, error, or read-only state. The owning component exposes and communicates those states. Icons do not announce changes; use visible text and the appropriate alert, toast, progress, or field semantics.

Accessible labels are application copy and must be translated. The artwork itself is not localised. Test directional icons in both `ltr` and `rtl` compositions because icons are not automatically mirrored.

Do not infer availability of any Lucide glyph that is not listed in the catalogue. New icons require a Breeze public API decision. Do not inspect or target generated SVG paths, classes, or underlying library names.

## Related components

- Use `IconTile` for a canonical semantic marker surface.
- Use `IconButton` for an icon-only action with an explicit accessible label.
- Use `Logo` for the brand mark and `Avatar` for identity.
- Use `Badge`, `Alert`, or `Typography` to state meaning in words.

    data-testid="information-notice"

      Primary application feedback
    </Alert>

    </Alert>

./src/primitives/Alert/Alert.mdx
Persistent inline feedback with an explicit assertive, polite, or non-live announcement policy.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Alert.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Persistent inline feedback with an explicit assertive, polite, or non-live announcement policy."
/>

# Alert

`Alert` displays persistent inline feedback and makes announcement urgency an explicit choice.

```tsx
import { Alert } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use an alert for feedback that should remain in the page near the affected context. Use [Toast](?path=/docs/feedback-toast--docs) for transient, provider-queued notifications, [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs) for a substantial empty or error state, and field `Error` parts for validation attached to a control.

Do not use an assertive alert for ordinary guidance already present at page load.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Alert } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SaveFeedback() {
  return (
    <Alert announcement="polite" variant="success">
      Your changes were saved.
    </Alert>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.InformationNotice} />

## Announcement behaviour

- `assertive` is the default. It renders `role="alert"`, `aria-live="assertive"`, and `aria-atomic="true"` for urgent newly inserted feedback.
- `polite` renders `role="status"`, `aria-live="polite"`, and `aria-atomic="true"` for non-urgent updates.
- `off` omits the live-region role and sets `aria-live="off"`; use it for persistent content already present when the page loads.

The DOM change must occur after the live region exists for reliable announcements. Do not duplicate the same message in another live region.

## Variants

`variant` accepts `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, or `dark`; the default is `info`. Choose by meaning, not decoration, and include text or an accessible icon so colour is never the only signal.

<Canvas of={Stories.Variants} />

## Accessibility, state, and direction

Alert has no keyboard interaction or internal controlled state. It does not own dismissal, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error logic; conditionally render it from application state and use `danger` for error meaning when appropriate. Translate its application-owned content. Its logical layout supports both directions; decorative icons should be hidden from assistive technology and meaningful icons need a text alternative without repeating the message.

`Alert` supports relevant native `div` attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `role` and inline `style` are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not leave `announcement="assertive"` on static informational content.
- Do not announce a validation error both in `Alert` and a field error.
- Do not put the only dismiss or recovery action in non-interactive text.
- Do not choose `success`, `warning`, or `danger` solely for visual emphasis.

## Related components

[Toast](?path=/docs/feedback-toast--docs), [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs), and compound field error parts cover transient feedback, page states, and validation.

./src/patterns/ApplicationShell/ApplicationShell.mdx
Responsive application frame that composes brand, navigation, context, account controls, skip navigation, and page content.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ApplicationShell.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Responsive application frame that composes brand, navigation, context, account controls, skip navigation, and page content."
/>

# ApplicationShell

`ApplicationShell` provides the shared responsive frame for a product application without owning routes, account state, or page content.

## Import

```tsx
import {
  ApplicationShell,
  Button,
  Logo,
  NavigationList,
  Typography,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it once around an authenticated application workspace that needs the canonical wide navigation rail and compact navigation drawer. Compose `NavigationList`, `ContextSwitcher`, and `UserMenu` into its slots.

Do not use it as a marketing-site header, repeat it per page, or put routing and authentication logic inside the shell. Use page-level `Container`, `PageHeader`, and layout primitives inside `children`; use `Drawer` directly for a separate temporary panel.

## Basic example

```tsx
import {
  ApplicationShell,
  Button,
  Logo,
  NavigationList,
  Typography,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/reset.css';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css';

export function Workspace() {
  return (
    <ApplicationShell
      account={
        <Button appearance="ghost" variant="light">
          Account
        </Button>
      }
      brand={<Logo size="lg" />}
      compactBrand={<Typography as="strong">Workspace</Typography>}
      navigation={
        <NavigationList.Root aria-label="Primary navigation">
          <NavigationList.Item current href="/overview" id="overview">
            Overview
          </NavigationList.Item>
          <NavigationList.Item href="/projects" id="projects">
            Projects
          </NavigationList.Item>
        </NavigationList.Root>
      }
      navigationLabel="Open navigation"
      skipLinkLabel="Skip to main content"
    >
      <Typography as="h1">Overview</Typography>
    </ApplicationShell>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.CompleteFrame} />

## Anatomy and responsive behaviour

The root contains a visible-on-focus skip link, a persistent rail from the `md` breakpoint, a compact header and start-edge drawer below `md`, and the labelled main-content target. `brand`, `navigation`, optional `context`, and `account` can be rendered in more than one responsive presentation, so their components must obtain changing state from the application and must not introduce a shared singleton DOM id. `compactBrand` defaults to `brand`.

The drawer uses logical `start`, so it opens from the left in left-to-right layouts and the right in right-to-left layouts. The shell supplies layout and landmarks; the application supplies destinations, the current route, translated product copy, context selection, account actions, loading/error content, and data fetching.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and routing

- `skipLinkLabel` must clearly describe bypassing repeated navigation. Its target is `mainId`, which defaults to `main-content`; keep that id unique on the page.
- `navigationLabel` names the compact drawer trigger and its dialog. It is application-owned, localised text.
- Use a labelled `NavigationList.Root` and set `current` from the active route. Local links use the `BreezeProvider` router adapter when configured; native anchors handle external URLs and native link behaviours.
- The drawer trigger and close action use normal button keyboard activation. Focus, `Escape` dismissal, focus containment, scroll locking, and restoration are supplied by `Drawer`.
- The main target has `tabIndex={-1}` so the skip link can move focus to it. Keep the first page heading inside `children`.

`ApplicationShell` has no disabled, read-only, invalid, loading, empty, or error mode. Render page feedback inside `children`, disable the relevant slotted controls themselves, and keep navigation and account recovery available whenever possible.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not put domain-specific page state in a shared shell slot.
- Do not render another `main` landmark inside `children`.
- Do not use physical left/right assumptions; direction is provider-controlled.
- Do not hard-code the shell breakpoint or rail offset in application CSS.
- Do not assume the frame performs route changes, signs users out, fetches contexts, or announces page loading.

## Related components

Use [ContextSwitcher](?path=/docs/patterns-application-shell-contextswitcher--docs) and [UserMenu](?path=/docs/patterns-application-shell-usermenu--docs) in the footer slots, [NavigationList](?path=/docs/navigation-navigationlist--docs) for destinations, [PageHeader](?path=/docs/patterns-page-structure-pageheader--docs) inside the page, and [SkipLink](?path=/docs/navigation-skiplink--docs) when building a different frame.

Deterministic initials fallback from a person name.
Omits `src` so Avatar derives the deterministic initials “AL” from the accessible person name; use this as the baseline fallback configuration.
const Initials = () => <Avatar name="Ada Lovelace" />;

Image avatar with named fallback behaviour.

  <Inline gap="md" wrap={false}>
    <Avatar name={name} size="sm" />
    <Avatar name={name} size="md" />
    <Avatar name={name} size="lg" />

    <Avatar initials="C" name="Compact entity" shape="square" size="sm" />
    <Avatar initials="P" name="Primary context" shape="square" size="md" />
    <Avatar

      name="Alternate context"
      shape="square"
      size="md"

./src/primitives/Avatar/Avatar.mdx
Accessible person or entity representation with a named image and deterministic initials fallback.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Avatar.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Accessible person or entity representation with a named image and deterministic initials fallback."
/>

# Avatar

`Avatar` represents a named person or entity with an optional image and a deterministic initials fallback.

## Import

```tsx
import { Avatar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

- Use a circular avatar for a person in account, participant, or authorship context.
- Use a square avatar for a compact non-person entity marker.
- Supply `src` only when an image is available; the same component handles loading failure by falling back to initials.

## When not to use

- Use `Logo` for the canonical Motech Development brand mark.
- Use `IconTile` for a semantic icon or short status marker that does not represent identity.
- Use ordinary content and `Typography` when the full name is the primary information; an Avatar should not replace necessary visible identity text.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Avatar, Inline, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function PersonSummary() {
  return (
    <Inline align="center" gap="sm" wrap={false}>
      <Avatar name="Alex Morgan" />
      <Typography as="span">Alex Morgan</Typography>
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Initials} />

## Image and fallback behaviour

When `src` is present, Avatar renders a cover-cropped image. The nested image has empty alternative text because the wrapper already exposes `name` once as a named image. If the resource fails to load, Avatar displays initials without changing its accessible name.

Without an `initials` override, Breeze trims `name`, takes the first character of the first two whitespace-separated words, uppercases them, and joins them. For example, “Alex Morgan” becomes “AM”. An explicit `initials` value is useful for an established short form or compact entity marker.

```tsx
import { Avatar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function EntityMarker() {
  return (
    <Avatar
      initials="EX"
      name="Example organisation"
      shape="square"
      tone="accent"
    />
  );
}
```

## Shape, size, and tone

- `circle` is the default and is preferred for people.
- `square` has square corners and is preferred for non-person entity markers.
- `sm` and `md` both occupy a 36-pixel square; `sm` uses smaller initials. `lg` occupies a 56-pixel square.
- `primary` is the default semantic treatment. `accent` provides a distinct alternate entity-marker treatment.

Tone applies to the initials fallback. A successfully loaded image fills the available geometry.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility, state, and internationalisation

Relevant native span attributes such as `id`, `aria-describedby`, and `data-*` are forwarded. `children` and arbitrary `style` are excluded so the image/fallback anatomy and canonical appearance remain stable.

Avatar exposes `role="img"` with `name` as its accessible name. Always provide the complete human-readable name even when visible initials are overridden. Place visible text beside the Avatar when users need to identify or distinguish similar people and entities; the accessible name alone is not visible context.

Avatar has no keyboard interaction and is not an action. Wrap the overall composition in an appropriate `Link` or `Button` only when it genuinely navigates or acts, and ensure that control has an unambiguous name.

Image availability is internal fallback state, not an application-controlled value. There are no loading, disabled, invalid, error-callback, or read-only props. Breeze does not localise names. Initial derivation uppercases with JavaScript's standard Unicode behaviour and does not implement locale-specific name parsing.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not omit `name`, even when `src` is supplied.
- Do not place a separate `alt` on the internal image; it is not public anatomy.
- Do not use a tone to communicate status; use `Badge`, `Alert`, or visible text.
- Keep explicit initials brief. Avatar does not truncate an arbitrary `initials` string.
- Avatar does not fetch profiles, upload images, indicate presence, or own account menus.

## Related components

- Use `Logo` for the Motech Development brand.
- Use `IconTile` for non-identity visual markers.
- Use `Badge` beside identity content for a compact classification or status.
- Use `UserMenu` for the account-action pattern that may compose an Avatar.

Badge

/**

./src/primitives/Badge/Badge.mdx
Compact non-interactive status or classification label with semantic colour and emphasis.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Badge.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Compact non-interactive status or classification label with semantic colour and emphasis."
/>

# Badge

`Badge` renders a compact, non-interactive status or classification label.

## Import

```tsx
import { Badge } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

- Use for a short status, category, count label, or classification adjacent to primary content.
- Choose `variant` for meaning and `appearance` for the amount of visual emphasis.
- Keep the label concise enough to scan as metadata.

## When not to use

- Use `Button`, `ToggleButton`, or `Tag` when the element is interactive.
- Use `Alert` for a message that needs explanatory content or announcement semantics.
- Use `Typography` for sentences and supporting prose.
- Use `Meter` or `ProgressBar` for a measurable value rather than encoding progress as badge colour.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Badge, Inline, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ItemStatus() {
  return (
    <Inline align="center" gap="sm" wrap={false}>
      <Typography as="span">Quarterly report</Typography>
      <Badge variant="success">Complete</Badge>
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.VariantsAndAppearances} />

## Variants

| Variant     | Intended use                                                          |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `primary`   | Current or product-primary classification.                            |
| `secondary` | Neutral metadata; the default.                                        |
| `success`   | Successful or complete state.                                         |
| `danger`    | Failed, destructive, or high-severity state.                          |
| `warning`   | State needing attention or caution.                                   |
| `info`      | Informational state.                                                  |
| `light`     | Low-emphasis label on an ordinary or dark-enough contrasting context. |
| `dark`      | Dark high-contrast label on a light context.                          |

The visible text must state the meaning; never rely on colour alone. Use one variant consistently for the same state within a product flow.

## Appearances

`subtle` is the default and works for most metadata. `solid` has the highest filled emphasis. `outline` uses a transparent background with a semantic border. `ghost` removes the visible border and background for the lightest treatment. Appearance does not change semantics or announce content.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## State, accessibility, and behaviour

Badge forwards relevant native span attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, `data-*`, and event-independent text attributes. Arbitrary inline `style` is not a public styling contract.

Badge is a plain non-interactive span. It has no keyboard behaviour and no controlled, uncontrolled, disabled, invalid, loading, empty, error, or read-only state. Empty badges provide no useful information and should not be rendered.

Dynamic Badge text is not automatically announced. If a change requires immediate assistive-technology attention, use the appropriate `Alert`, `Toast`, or application-owned live-region pattern rather than adding live semantics indiscriminately.

Keep wording understandable when colours are unavailable or forced-colour mode changes presentation. Badge content follows provider direction but is not translated or formatted by the component.

## Styling and composition

Badge has a canonical compact height, rounded geometry, display font, and content padding. Use `className` for placement in a parent layout, not to invent new variants, sizes, or shapes. Long content wraps, but a label that becomes a sentence belongs in Typography or Alert.

Icons may accompany text when they reinforce meaning, but keep them decorative and retain a visible label. An icon-only Badge is generally ambiguous.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not attach click handlers or present Badge as a control.
- Do not use `danger`, `warning`, or `success` without explicit wording.
- Do not put lengthy explanations inside the compact label.
- Do not use appearance as a state model; it controls emphasis only.
- Badge does not own counts, status transitions, or announcements.

## Related components

- Use `Tag` for an individual interactive or removable item.
- Use `Alert` for explanatory feedback.
- Use `IconTile` for a larger visual marker.
- Use `Typography` for ordinary metadata and prose.

BreezeProvider

Establishes the locale, direction, messages, routing, portal, and toast environment required by every Breeze component subtree.
summary: required environment boundary for every Breeze component
Prop types (react-docgen-typescript) 8 prop types 
Component: src/provider/BreezeProvider.tsx::BreezeProvider
Props:
/**
 * Breeze components and application content rendered inside the boundary.
 */
children: ReactNode

/**
 * Reading direction override. Defaults to the direction inferred from `locale`.
 */
direction?: BreezeDirection

/**
 * BCP 47 locale used by Breeze formatting and accessible interactions.
 */
locale: string

/**
 * Partial replacements for the built-in `en-GB` generic messages.
 */
messages?: Partial<BreezeMessages>

/**
 * Portal host for layered content. Defaults to a provider-owned host.
 */
portalContainer?: HTMLElement | null

/**
 * Optional router adapter used for internal client-side navigation.
 */
router?: BreezeRouterAdapter

/**
 * Optional IANA time-zone name used by date and time formatting components.
 */
timeZone?: string

/**
 * Maximum visible provider-scoped toasts before pending items queue. Defaults to `3`.
 */
toastLimit?: number = 3

Explicit Right To Left story ok

./src/provider/BreezeProvider.mdx
Required environment boundary for Breeze locale, direction, messages, routing, portals, and notifications.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './BreezeProvider.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Required environment boundary for Breeze locale, direction, messages, routing, portals, and notifications."
/>

# BreezeProvider

`BreezeProvider` is the required environment boundary for every Breeze component. It establishes locale, reading direction, translated library messages, routing, portal placement, time-zone preferences, and the provider-scoped toast queue.

## Import

```tsx
import {
  BreezeProvider,
  type BreezeMessageOverrides,
  type BreezeRouterAdapter,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

Import the compiled component styles once at the application entry point. A greenfield application may opt into the reset before the component styles.

```tsx
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/reset.css';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css';
```

## When to use

- Wrap each independently rendered Breeze application root once.
- Supply the user's BCP 47 locale and, where temporal values require it, their IANA time-zone name.
- Add a router adapter when local Breeze links should use client-side navigation.
- Override `messages` when an application localises Breeze-owned generic text and announcements.

## When not to use

- Do not nest providers merely to style a subsection; Breeze has one canonical theme and typed component variants.
- Do not use it as application state, authentication, data-fetching, or form context. Keep those concerns in application-owned providers.
- Do not mount a separate provider around each overlay or toast. The nearest application provider already owns their shared environment.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { BreezeProvider, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import '@motech-development/breeze-ui/styles.css';

export function Application() {
  return (
    <BreezeProvider locale="en-GB" timeZone="Europe/London">
      <Typography as="h1">Account settings</Typography>
    </BreezeProvider>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.LeftToRight} />

## Environment and behaviour

The provider renders a `data-breeze-root` element with `lang` and `dir`. When `direction` is omitted, Breeze infers `ltr` or `rtl` from `locale`; an explicit direction always wins. Use a BCP 47 locale such as `en-GB`, `fr-FR`, or `ar-SA`, not an application-specific language key.

When `portalContainer` is omitted, Breeze creates a provider-owned portal host in `document.body` and applies the same locale and direction to it. Pass a stable `HTMLElement` only when overlays must live in an application-owned host. Portal creation is guarded for server rendering.

The provider also owns the toast region. At most `toastLimit` toasts are visible; additional toasts queue. `toastLimit` must be a positive integer and throws a `RangeError` otherwise.

## Routing

The adapter is deliberately small:

```tsx
import {
  BreezeProvider,
  Link,
  type BreezeRouterAdapter,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

const router: BreezeRouterAdapter = {
  navigate(href) {
    window.history.pushState(null, '', href);
  },
};

export function RoutedApplication() {
  return (
    <BreezeProvider locale="en-GB" router={router}>
      <Link href="/preferences">Preferences</Link>
    </BreezeProvider>
  );
}
```

Link-capable components use `router.navigate(href)` for an unmodified primary activation of a local `/path`. External URLs, downloads, alternate targets, and modified clicks retain native browser behaviour. The application remains responsible for updating its rendered route after navigation.

## Messages

`messages` is a partial override. Unspecified entries retain the built-in domain-neutral `en-GB` text.

```tsx
import type { ReactNode } from 'react';
import {
  BreezeProvider,
  type BreezeMessageOverrides,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

const messages: BreezeMessageOverrides = {
  clear: 'Effacer',
  close: 'Fermer',
  loading: 'Chargement',
  noResults: 'Aucun résultat',
  notifications: 'Notifications',
};

export function LocalisedApplication({ children }: { children: ReactNode }) {
  return (
    <BreezeProvider locale="fr-FR" messages={messages}>
      {children}
    </BreezeProvider>
  );
}
```

The complete catalogue is `clear`, `close`, `fileCountRejection`, `fileSizeRejection`, `fileTypeRejection`, `hidePassword`, `loading`, `nextPage`, `noResults`, `notifications`, `page`, `previousPage`, `selectDate`, `selectDateTime`, `selectFiles`, `selectTime`, `showPassword`, and `unreadNotifications`. Translate meaning rather than control-specific wording, and keep accessible labels concise.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and keyboard interaction

The provider itself adds no keyboard commands. Its locale and direction feed the keyboard, focus, and announcement behaviour of descendant controls. Keep one consistent locale and direction for a coherent subtree, translate the message catalogue, and do not remove the provider-owned portal root from the document while an overlay is open.

Setting `direction="rtl"` affects logical layout and interaction direction without changing the language. Use an explicit override for testing or exceptional mixed-language surfaces; otherwise prefer locale inference.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Rendering any Breeze component outside `BreezeProvider` throws an error.
- `locale` is required even when the application only uses visual primitives.
- `timeZone` must be an IANA name such as `Europe/London`, not an offset such as `+01:00`.
- Do not recreate built-in toast or portal infrastructure in each feature.
- Do not pass an element that is replaced on every render as `portalContainer`.
- Message overrides localise Breeze-owned generic UI only; application copy remains application-owned.

## Related components

- Use `Link` and `LinkButton` with `router` for router-neutral navigation.
- Use `FormattedDateTime`, `FormattedNumber`, `FormattedList`, and `RelativeTime` for provider-locale display formatting.
- Use `Toast` and `ToastRegion` for notifications owned by this provider boundary.

Button

Performs an application action through pointer or keyboard activation.
Prop types (react-docgen-typescript) 10 prop types 
Component: src/primitives/Button/Button.tsx::Button
Props:
/**
 * Visual emphasis treatment. Defaults to `solid`.
 */
appearance?: ButtonAppearance

/**
 * Button content.
 */
children: ReactNode

/**
 * Placement and composition classes.
 */
className?: string

/**
 * Prevents interaction. Defaults to `false`.
 */
disabled?: boolean = false

/**
 * Shows and announces an in-progress state while preventing activation. Defaults to `false`.
 */
loading?: boolean = false

/**
 * Called once when the user activates the button.
 */
onAction?: (() => void)

/**
 * Ref to the rendered button.
 */
ref?: Ref<HTMLButtonElement>

/**
 * Canonical control size. Defaults to `md`.
 */
size?: ControlSize

/**
 * Constrained native form behavior: `button` or `submit`. Defaults to `button`.
 */
type?: NativeButtonType = button

/**
 * Semantic colour. Defaults to `primary`.
 */
variant?: VisualVariant

    <Button disabled>Disabled</Button>
    <Button loading>Saving</Button>

Native Form Behaviors story ok
Constrained native button and submit form behaviours.
Breeze deliberately supports only safe `button` and `submit` form behavior.
const NativeFormBehaviors = () => (
  <form onSubmit={(event) => event.preventDefault()}>
    <Stack gap="compact">
      <TextField.Root defaultValue="Initial value">
        <TextField.Label>Example value</TextField.Label>
        <TextField.Input />
      </TextField.Root>
      <Inline gap="compact">
        <Button type="button">Button</Button>
        <Button type="submit">Submit</Button>
      </Inline>
    </Stack>
  </form>
);
Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/Button/Button.mdx
A semantic application action with canonical emphasis, sizing, and accessible progress states.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Button.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A semantic application action with canonical emphasis, sizing, and accessible progress states."
/>

# Button

`Button` performs an application action through pointer or keyboard activation.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Button` to submit a form or change application state. Use `Link` for ordinary navigation, `LinkButton` when a destination needs button emphasis, `IconButton` for a compact icon-only action, and `ToggleButton` for a persistent on/off choice.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SaveButton() {
  return <Button onAction={() => saveChanges()}>Save changes</Button>;
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Activation} />

## Emphasis and size

Choose `appearance` for emphasis and `variant` for meaning. `solid` is the default primary action; `subtle`, `outline`, and `ghost` progressively reduce emphasis. `text` is the lowest-emphasis button treatment and remains an action, never navigation. Semantic variants are `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, and `dark`. Avoid relying on colour alone; use clear action text.

Sizes are `sm`, `md`, and `lg`. Prefer `md`; use `sm` only in space-constrained contexts that retain an adequate target, and `lg` for deliberately prominent actions.

```tsx
<Button appearance="outline" variant="danger" onAction={removeItem}>
  Remove item
</Button>
```

## Activation, forms, and state

`onAction` runs once for a semantic activation, independent of whether the user clicked, touched, or used the keyboard. Use it instead of `onClick`. `type` is deliberately limited to `button` and `submit`, and defaults to the safe non-submitting `button`.

`disabled` prevents interaction. `loading` prevents activation while preserving focusability, sets `aria-busy`, and adds an announced in-progress state while retaining the visible label. Keep the label stable, such as “Save changes”, or use a short progressive label such as “Saving”. Errors and validation remain owned by the surrounding form or feedback component; `Button` has no invalid, error, read-only, controlled, or empty state.

<Canvas of={Stories.States} />

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and keyboard behaviour

Relevant native button attributes such as `name`, `value`, `form`, `autoFocus`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported. Breeze owns `onClick`, `disabled`, `type`, `aria-busy`, `className`, and inline styling through the API above.

Use a concise visible label; when an icon accompanies text, keep the icon decorative. `Enter` and `Space` activate the button when enabled, with browser and React Aria press semantics preventing duplicate callbacks. Focus remains visibly indicated. Do not remove focus styles or encode meaning using variant alone. Labels may expand after translation, so allow action layouts to wrap rather than truncating essential text.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `Button` to navigate or pass an anchor destination; use `Link` or `LinkButton`.
- Do not attach both `onAction` and a native click handler.
- Do not put more than one primary solid action in the same decision group without a clear hierarchy.
- Do not replace a useful label with only an icon; use `IconButton` with `aria-label`.

## Related components

Use `LinkButton` for destinations, `IconButton` for icon-only actions, `ToggleButton` for persistent two-state actions, `ButtonGroup` for grouped actions, and `FormActions` for canonical form placement.

ButtonGroup

./src/patterns/ButtonGroup/ButtonGroup.mdx
Responsive semantic grouping for related application actions, with logical alignment and a mobile-first visual flow that never changes DOM or keyboard order.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ButtonGroup.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Responsive semantic grouping for related application actions, with logical alignment and a mobile-first visual flow that never changes DOM or keyboard order."
/>

# ButtonGroup

`ButtonGroup` arranges a small set of related actions with consistent spacing, alignment and responsive flow.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, ButtonGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `ButtonGroup` when two or more `Button`, `LinkButton` or `IconButton` controls belong to the same local task. It provides visual layout and a semantic group without owning the actions.

Do not use it for form submission ordering; use `FormActions`. Use `Toolbar` when arrow-key navigation should coordinate a collection of commands. Use `ToggleGroup` or `SegmentedControl` when the controls share selection state.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, ButtonGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<ButtonGroup
  aria-label="Item actions"
  orientation={{ base: 'verticalReverse', sm: 'horizontal' }}
>
  <Button appearance="outline" variant="secondary" onAction={cancelChanges}>
    Cancel
  </Button>
  <Button onAction={saveChanges}>Save changes</Button>
</ButtonGroup>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Responsive} />

## Anatomy and responsive behaviour

`ButtonGroup` is a single `div` with `role="group"`. Its children remain ordinary Breeze actions and retain their own button, link, loading, disabled and callback semantics.

`orientation` accepts a scalar value or a mobile-first responsive object. A responsive object requires `base` and may override it at `sm` (681px), `md` (901px) and `lg` (1181px).

| Orientation       | Behaviour                                                                                                |
| ----------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `horizontal`      | Actions flow in DOM order and wrap when space is constrained.                                            |
| `vertical`        | Actions stack in DOM order and stretch across the available width.                                       |
| `verticalReverse` | Actions stack in reverse **visual** order and stretch; DOM, focus and screen-reader order do not change. |

The canonical compact form arrangement places a secondary action first in JSX, then uses `{ base: 'verticalReverse', sm: 'horizontal' }` so the primary action is visually first on compact screens and trailing on wider screens. Keep the source order meaningful because Tab navigation always follows the DOM.

## Alignment, state and callbacks

`align` is scalar and defaults to `start`. Choose `start`, `center`, `end` or `spaceBetween`; start and end are logical directions and therefore adapt to right-to-left layouts.

ButtonGroup has no controlled or uncontrolled value, read-only state, loading state, validation, error or empty presentation. Configure `disabled`, `loading`, `onAction`, `href` and routing behaviour on each child. Avoid rendering an empty group.

## Accessibility, keyboard and internationalisation

- Supply `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` when the relationship between the actions is not already clear from nearby content. The default group role does not create an accessible name by itself.
- ButtonGroup adds no arrow-key model. Tab and Shift+Tab move through children in DOM order; each child keeps its own Enter, Space or link behaviour.
- Keep labels concise, translated and able to wrap. Provider direction controls logical alignment; do not reverse the child array for right-to-left content.
- Icon-only children still require their own accessible names. Do not communicate the primary action through visual position alone.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not reorder JSX at each breakpoint, assume visual order changes keyboard order, place unrelated commands together, or use `spaceBetween` to create page layout. `FormActions` owns canonical form-action priority, `Toolbar` owns command navigation, and `ToggleGroup` owns shared selection.

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Center.stories';

<Meta

/>

# Center

`Center` centres its content horizontally and vertically, optionally within at least the viewport height.

## Import

```tsx
import { Center } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Center` for a bounded empty, loading, sign-in, or status composition that should sit on both axes. Use `Container` to centre and bound page width without vertical centring, or `Stack align="center"` when only cross-axis alignment is needed.

## Basic example

```tsx
import {
  Center,
  Spinner,
  Stack,
  Typography,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function LoadingView() {
  return (
    <Center minHeight="screen">
      <Stack align="center" gap="sm">
        <Spinner label="Loading results" />
        <Typography>Loading results…</Typography>
      </Stack>
    </Center>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Content} />

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and responsive behaviour

Relevant native `div` attributes including `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported; inline `style` is not. `Center` has no callbacks or controlled, disabled, invalid, read-only, loading, empty, or error state of its own.

The wrapper is a neutral `div` with no role, focus, label, or keyboard behaviour. Give loading and status content its own accessible semantics. `minHeight="screen"` is a minimum rather than a fixed height, so zoomed, translated, or error content can grow and remain reachable. Centring is unchanged in right-to-left direction.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `Center` to hide overflowing content or force a fixed viewport height.
- Do not centre long prose, dense forms, or whole application pages when start alignment is easier to scan.
- Do not assume it supplies live-region semantics for loading or errors.

## Related components

Use `StatePanel` for a standard empty/error composition, `Spinner` for announced progress, `Stack` inside the centred region, and `Container` for normal page gutters.

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

/>

# ConfirmationDialog

`ConfirmationDialog` presents a focused, consequential decision with canonical warning anatomy and a safe initial focus target.

## Import

```tsx
import { ConfirmationDialog } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `ConfirmationDialog` before a destructive, risky or materially consequential action when the standard title, consequence, cancel and confirm structure is sufficient.

## When not to use

Use compound `AlertDialog` when you need custom content, more than two decision actions, custom close labelling or direct styling. Use `Dialog` for routine modal tasks, `Alert` for inline feedback and `Toast` for non-blocking status. Do not ask for confirmation for harmless, easily reversible actions.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { ConfirmationDialog } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<ConfirmationDialog
  cancelLabel="Keep item"
  closeLabel="Close confirmation"
  confirmLabel="Delete item"
  description="The item and its attached files will be permanently removed."
  onConfirm={deleteItem}
  title="Delete this item?"
  trigger="Delete item"
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Destructive} />

## Anatomy

ConfirmationDialog is a fixed composition rather than a public compound namespace.

| Region      | Responsibility                                                            |
| ----------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Trigger     | A semantic Breeze button that opens the alert dialog.                     |
| Header      | Variant-coloured warning icon, required title and icon-only close action. |
| Description | Required consequence or decision context and the accessible description.  |
| Actions     | A cancel action focused on open and a confirming action.                  |

The icon is supplementary; meaning must be present in the title, description and action labels. On compact screens the actions stack in a safe visual order. The complete structure is deliberately not replaceable through slots.

## Variants and action state

`variant` accepts `'danger'`, `'warning'` or `'primary'` and defaults to `'danger'`. It colours the trigger, icon and confirming action. `triggerAppearance` accepts `'solid'`, `'subtle'`, `'outline'` or `'ghost'` and defaults to the trigger Button’s solid appearance.

`disabled` disables only the confirming action. Cancel, the header close action and Escape remain available. The pattern has no loading prop: keep asynchronous state application-owned, prevent duplicate confirmation through `disabled` when needed, and present progress or failure outside this fixed composition or use `AlertDialog` for a richer workflow.

## Open state and callbacks

- **Uncontrolled:** omit `open`; use optional `defaultOpen` and `onOpenChange`.
- **Controlled:** provide `open` and `onOpenChange`. The callback receives the complete next Boolean after trigger activation, confirmation, cancellation, the close action or Escape.
- **Read-only:** provide `open` with `readOnly`. Requests cannot change the immutable state; this is useful for a fixed presentation, not a normal decision flow.

`onConfirm` takes no event and runs once when the enabled confirming action is explicitly activated. That action also requests the dialog to close. The application owns the mutation, optimistic state, persistence, server errors and recovery. Do not combine `defaultOpen` with `open`. Providing mutable `open` without `onOpenChange` fails the TypeScript contract and throws at runtime.

## Keyboard, focus and accessibility

- Enter or Space activates the trigger and action buttons.
- Opening moves focus to Cancel. Tab and Shift+Tab remain contained within the modal surface.
- Escape requests closure. Outside pointer interaction does not dismiss the alert dialog.
- Closing restores focus to the trigger when it remains mounted.
- `title` must name the decision. `description` must explain the consequence rather than repeat the title. Prefer specific labels such as “Delete item” and “Keep item” over “Yes” and “No”.
- Keep a usable non-confirming route. Never focus the destructive action initially or rely on colour to convey risk.

All visible decision copy and the icon-only `closeLabel` are application-owned and should be translated together. Layout uses logical direction and permits wrapping.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not start the destructive operation from the trigger, use confirmation as an error message, hide the consequence in vague copy, disable every escape route, or treat `variant` as a severity value for business logic. Use `AlertDialog` for bespoke decisions, `Dialog` for non-consequential modal work, and `FormActions` to place the trigger in a form layout.

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Container.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A bounded application workspace with responsive page gutters."
/>

# Container

`Container` centres application content in a full-width workspace, limits it to the Breeze maximum width, and applies mobile-first page gutters.

## Import

```tsx
import { Container } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use one `Container` around page-level content that should share the standard maximum width and gutters. Use `Stack`, `Inline`, or `Grid` inside it to arrange content.

Do not use `Container` for a visually bounded panel; use `Surface` or `Card` instead. Do not nest containers merely to add spacing, because their maximum width and automatic inline margins are page-level behaviour.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Container, Stack, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function AccountPage() {
  return (
    <Container>
      <Stack gap="xl">
        <Typography as="h1" level="heading-xl">
          Account
        </Typography>
        <Typography>Manage your account details.</Typography>
      </Stack>
    </Container>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ResponsiveWorkspace} />

## Responsive gutters

`padding` accepts one spacing value or an object with a required `base` value and optional `sm`, `md`, and `lg` overrides. Breakpoints are mobile-first: `sm` starts at 681 px, `md` at 901 px, and `lg` at 1181 px. The default is `{ base: 'md', sm: 'xl', md: 'xxl', lg: 'page' }`.

```tsx
<Container padding={{ base: 'sm', md: 'page' }}>Page content</Container>
```

Spacing values are `none`, `xs`, `sm`, `control`, `compact`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl`, and `page`. Prefer the default gutters unless a documented page composition needs another token.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and behaviour

`Container` also accepts relevant native `div` attributes, including `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*`; inline `style` is intentionally unavailable. It has no loading, disabled, invalid, empty, controlled, or read-only state.

`Container` is a neutral `div`: it adds no landmark, label, focus, or keyboard behaviour. Put semantic elements such as `main`, `nav`, and headings inside it. If the container itself is labelled, ensure the label communicates a real relationship rather than presentation. Content order remains the authored DOM order at every breakpoint and direction.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `className` to recreate arbitrary page gutters when `padding` covers the requirement.
- Do not assume `Container` supplies a `main` landmark.
- Do not reorder children visually at breakpoints.

## Related components

Use `Stack` for vertical flow, `Grid` for responsive columns, `Center` for two-axis centring, and `Surface` for a coloured or bordered inset region.

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ContextSwitcher.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Controlled, keyboard-complete menu for selecting one application-owned context by stable string id."
/>

# ContextSwitcher

`ContextSwitcher` lets a person move between workspaces, organisations, or another application-defined context.

## Import

```tsx
import { Avatar, ContextSwitcher } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it when most application content is scoped to exactly one current context and context changes deserve a persistent shell control. Use `Select` for a form value, `Menu` for unrelated commands, or `NavigationList` when every option is a destination rather than a global context change.

The application owns the context collection, loading and error handling, authorisation, confirmation of destructive switches, persistence, and any route change after selection.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Avatar, ContextSwitcher } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<ContextSwitcher
  aria-label="Switch workspace"
  currentId="design"
  items={[
    {
      description: 'Primary workspace',
      icon: <Avatar initials="D" name="Design team" shape="square" size="md" />,
      id: 'design',
      name: 'Design team',
    },
    { id: 'research', name: 'Research team' },
  ]}
  manageLabel="Manage workspaces"
  onChange={(id) => setCurrentWorkspaceId(id)}
  onManage={() => navigate('/workspaces')}
  triggerLabel="Current workspace"
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Selection} />

## State, items, and callbacks

This is a controlled pattern. `currentId` is a string id from `items`, or `null` when a selection is required; `onChange(id)` reports the requested next id and receives no DOM event. Update `currentId` after application validation succeeds. There is no `defaultCurrentId` or read-only mode. Disable unavailable items individually; when switching is wholly unavailable, render a non-interactive context summary.

Each `ContextSwitcherItem` requires a stable `id` and plain-string `name`. `name` supplies visible text and menu typeahead. `description` and `icon` add supporting context. `disabled` prevents that item from receiving selection or action. Item order is preserved.

When `currentId` is `null` or does not match an item, the trigger shows `emptyIcon` and `emptyName`, falling back to the `aria-label`. The optional management row appears only when both `manageLabel` and `onManage` are supplied. Its internal collision-safe key is not public and must not be referenced.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalisation

`aria-label` names both trigger and menu; provide a concise translated action such as “Switch workspace”. `triggerLabel`, names, descriptions, empty copy, and management copy are application-owned and must be localised. Names should remain distinguishable without relying on icons, which should be decorative unless they convey information absent from the text.

The trigger opens with pointer activation, `Enter`, `Space`, or the platform menu shortcut. Arrow keys move through enabled items, typing moves by `name`, `Enter` or `Space` selects, `Home` and `End` move to boundaries, and `Escape` closes and restores trigger focus. Behaviour comes from the composed `Menu`. Logical alignment and `top start` placement follow provider direction.

The pattern does not fetch items or display loading, invalid, or error feedback. Show an appropriate `Skeleton`, `Spinner`, `Alert`, or `StatePanel` outside it, and avoid presenting an unexplained empty menu.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use array position as an id, pass a stale `currentId` silently, place an essential distinction only in colour, or perform the context change without updating controlled state. Related components: [ApplicationShell](?path=/docs/patterns-application-shell-applicationshell--docs), [Menu](?path=/docs/navigation-menu--docs), [Select](?path=/docs/forms-select--docs), and [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs).

/>

# FileAttachment

`FileAttachment` presents one selected file name with persistent application-owned actions.

## Import

```tsx
import {
  Button,
  FileAttachment,
  LinkButton,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `FileAttachment` after selection or upload when a file must remain visible beside actions such as view, download, replace or remove. It is a presentation pattern for a native `File` or application record that your application already owns.

## When not to use

Use `FileUpload` to select files, `FileTrigger` for a compact native chooser, and `DropZone` when drag and drop is the primary affordance. Use a collection component when many files need selection, sorting or bulk actions. FileAttachment does not upload, preview, validate or remove anything.

## Basic example

```tsx
import {
  Button,
  FileAttachment,
  LinkButton,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<FileAttachment
  name="project-brief.pdf"
  actions={
    <>
      <LinkButton appearance="outline" href="/files/project-brief.pdf">
        View file
      </LinkButton>
      <Button variant="danger" onAction={removeFile}>
        Remove file
      </Button>
    </>
  }
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.WithActions} />

## Anatomy and responsive behaviour

| Region        | Responsibility                                                    |
| ------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------- |
| File identity | A decorative paperclip and persistent, visually truncated `name`. |
| Actions       | One or more application-owned controls in logical source order.   |

On compact layouts the row stacks and each direct action stretches to the available width. From the `sm` breakpoint the file name and wrapping actions share a row. The name region can shrink without forcing page overflow; the full string remains in the DOM for assistive technology.

The root has no role by default. If the row needs its own accessible grouping semantics, pass `role="group"` with `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`. Give every action a specific accessible name, especially icon-only controls. The paperclip is decorative and must not supply meaning.

## State, callbacks and routing

FileAttachment is stateless. `name` is display content rather than a path, URL or native `File`; `actions` is rendered without inspection. Configure `onAction`, `href`, downloads, loading, disabled state, confirmation and routing on the child controls.

There is no empty, invalid, read-only, loading, error or progress mode. Omit the row when no file exists. Pair it with `Alert`, `ProgressBar` or other feedback when the application needs upload progress or failure recovery. Applications own file records, object URL lifecycle, authorisation, storage and deletion.

## Accessibility, internationalisation and styling

- Use the recognisable file name or another concise visible identifier. Do not provide an unlabeled icon as `name`.
- Translate action labels and allow them to wrap. Filenames are user or system data and should not be translated.
- Layout uses logical ordering and inherits provider direction. Keep actions in the intended keyboard order rather than reversing arrays for right-to-left presentation.
- `className` is for placement and width composition. Do not depend on internal `data-*` attributes or override the row’s responsive geometry.
- A `LinkButton` child uses the provider router adapter for eligible local navigation and preserves native external, download and modified-click behaviour. FileAttachment itself performs no routing.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass a `File` object directly as `name`, assume the pattern starts or retries uploads, hide destructive action meaning behind an icon, or render an empty actions region. Use `FileUpload`, `FileTrigger` or `DropZone` for selection, and require `ConfirmationDialog` when removal has a material consequence.

FileUpload

Selects files through browse or drop while leaving upload transport application-owned.

/**

 */

 * Called with files rejected by Breeze constraints.
 */
onReject?: ((rejections: FileRejection[]) => void)

/**
 * Selected files owned by the application and presented as feedback.
 */
selectedFiles?: readonly File[] = []
Imports

Browse And Drop story ok
keyboard-accessible browse and drop file selection
Combines a focusable drop target and native browse control, validates the authored file constraints, and reports accepted files without uploading.
const BrowseAndDrop = () => <FileUpload
    acceptedFileTypes={['application/pdf', 'image/jpeg', 'image/png']}
    browseLabel="Browse"
    guidance="PDF, JPG or PNG"
    label="No file selected"
    onFiles={fn()} />;
Selected Files story ok
application-owned selected files with replacement action
Replaces the empty prompt with locale-aware selected file names while the application retains the files and owns replacement or upload progress.
const SelectedFiles = () => <FileUpload
    browseLabel="Replace files"
    guidance="PDF, JPG or PNG"
    label="Add supporting files"
    onFiles={fn()}
    selectedFiles={[
      new File(['one'], 'brief.pdf', { type: 'application/pdf' }),
      new File(['two'], 'diagram.png', { type: 'image/png' }),
    ]} />;

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

/>

# FileUpload

`FileUpload` combines a focusable drop target and native browse button into one accessible file-selection surface.

## Import

```tsx
import { FileUpload, type FileRejection } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `FileUpload` for the canonical browse-and-drop attachment entry when the application can own selected files and upload processing. Drag and drop is optional convenience; the browse button is the keyboard and touch route.

## When not to use

Use compound `FileTrigger` for a compact chooser, directory or camera capture, native form naming, or required state. Use `DropZone` and `FileTrigger` directly when you need custom anatomy, a visible application error, drop read-failure handling, refs or placement classes. Use `FileAttachment` to present actions for an accepted or uploaded file.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { FileUpload, type FileRejection } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { useState } from 'react';

export function SupportingFiles() {
  const [files, setFiles] = useState<File[]>([]);

  const handleReject = (rejections: FileRejection[]) => {
    reportRejectedFiles(rejections);
  };

  return (
    <FileUpload
      acceptedFileTypes={['application/pdf', 'image/*']}
      allowsMultiple
      browseLabel="Browse files"
      guidance="PDF or image files, up to 10 MB each"
      label="Drop files here"
      maxFileSize={10 * 1024 * 1024}
      onFiles={setFiles}
      onReject={handleReject}
      selectedFiles={files}
    />
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.BrowseAndDrop} />

## Anatomy

FileUpload is a fixed pattern rather than a public compound namespace.

| Region          | Responsibility                                                                      |
| --------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Drop surface    | Focus, drag state, persistent label, optional guidance and dropped-file validation. |
| Browse action   | Opens the browser’s native file chooser with the same constraints.                  |
| Selection label | Shows `label` while empty, then locale-formats `selectedFiles` names as a list.     |
| Feedback        | Announces Breeze-owned type, size and count rejections from either selection route. |

The application must feed accepted files back through `selectedFiles`; FileUpload does not retain them as upload state. Replacing the prop with a new array updates the displayed names. It does not render per-file actions or progress.

## File formats and callback semantics

`acceptedFileTypes` accepts exact MIME types such as `'application/pdf'`, wildcard groups such as `'image/*'`, and dot-prefixed extensions such as `'.csv'`. Matching is case-insensitive. `maxFileSize` is bytes. `allowsMultiple` defaults to `false`; files after the first are rejected with `'file-count'`.

`onFiles` receives the accepted native `File[]` after browse or drop validation, including an empty array when no selected file passes. `onReject` runs when constraints reject files and receives `FileRejection[]`. Each record has the native `file` and every applicable reason:

| Reason         | Meaning                                                                |
| -------------- | ---------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `'file-count'` | The file exceeds the single-file count when `allowsMultiple` is false. |
| `'file-size'`  | `file.size` exceeds `maxFileSize`.                                     |
| `'file-type'`  | MIME type or extension does not match `acceptedFileTypes`.             |

Mixed selections deliver accepted files to `onFiles` and rejected records to `onReject`. Client constraints are usability guidance, not a security boundary; the application and server must validate again. Upload transport, storage, progress, retry, scanning, persistence and business rules remain application-owned.

## Empty, disabled, invalid and error behaviour

- `selectedFiles` defaults to `[]`; the visible `label` is the empty prompt. There is no separate empty-content slot.
- `disabled` prevents both drop and browse interaction and visibly reduces emphasis.
- `invalid` exposes invalid state on the drop target and hidden file input. The fixed pattern has no application error prop; render associated recovery guidance outside it or compose `DropZone.Error` directly.
- Built-in feedback covers only Breeze count, type and size rejections. FileUpload does not expose `DropZone`’s native `onFileReadError`; use the primitives directly when dropped-item read failures must be reported.
- There is no controlled value, read-only state or loading prop. Disable replacement only when necessary and render upload progress separately.

## Keyboard, labelling and internationalisation

- Tab can focus the drop surface and then the browse button. Enter or Space on Browse opens the native chooser. The operating system owns interaction inside the chooser and restores focus afterwards.
- `label` is persistent and names the drop target; `browseLabel` names the button. Keep both visible and specific. `guidance` should state relevant type, size and count constraints.
- Dragging must never be the only selection route. Do not rely on active border colour or file-extension filtering alone.
- Selected filenames are joined with `FormattedList` using the `BreezeProvider` locale. Filenames and native `File` values are not translated. Visible copy inherits provider direction and must tolerate expansion.
- Built-in rejection feedback currently uses fixed English copy. Compose the lower-level file primitives and provide application-owned `onReject` feedback when those messages must be localised.
- FileUpload performs no routing and accepts no deep-imported file types.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not expect `selectedFiles` to upload files, express byte limits as decimal prose without deciding the actual byte value, use client validation as security, translate filenames, or hide rejection feedback. Use `FileTrigger` and `DropZone` for lower-level control, `FileAttachment` for persistent file actions, and `Alert` or `ProgressBar` for application upload status.

FormActions

 */

 */

 */

 */
secondary?: ReactNode

    <Stack data-testid="full-height-form" gap="lg">
      <Typography>Form content</Typography>
      <FormActions primary={primary} />
    </Stack>
  </StoryConstraint>
);

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';

  of={Stories}
  summary="Canonical responsive form-action region that keeps primary, back, cancel, secondary and destructive responsibilities in a consistent compact and wide visual order."
/>

# FormActions

`FormActions` places primary and supporting form actions in a consistent responsive priority order.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, FormActions } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `FormActions` at the end of a form or form-like task when primary, cancel, back, secondary or destructive actions need the canonical compact and wide arrangement.

## When not to use

Use `ButtonGroup` for a general related action set whose order you control directly. Use `Toolbar` for a command collection with arrow-key navigation. FormActions does not submit, reset, navigate, confirm destructive work or manage form state; the supplied actions own those behaviours.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, FormActions } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<form onSubmit={saveItem}>
  {/* Labelled form fields */}
  <FormActions
    aria-label="Form actions"
    cancel={
      <Button appearance="outline" onAction={cancelEditing}>
        Cancel
      </Button>
    }
    danger={
      <Button variant="danger" onAction={requestRemoval}>
        Delete item
      </Button>
    }
    primary={<Button type="submit">Save changes</Button>}
  />
</form>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.CompleteSet} />

## Anatomy and ordering

FormActions exposes named action slots rather than public compound parts.

| Slot        | Purpose                                | Compact visual position | Wide position                        |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------- | ----------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| `primary`   | Required submit or continuation action | First                   | Trailing edge                        |
| `back`      | Move to the preceding step             | After primary           | Leading edge                         |
| `cancel`    | Abandon or leave the task              | After back              | Start of trailing group              |
| `secondary` | Additional non-primary action          | After cancel            | Before primary in the trailing group |
| `danger`    | Destructive action                     | Last                    | Leading edge                         |

Source order remains danger, back, cancel, secondary, primary; compact CSS changes only the visual order. Tab navigation and assistive technology follow source order, so labels and task context must make priority clear without relying on position alone. Omit unused slots rather than rendering empty wrappers.

## Layout, state and callbacks

`divided` defaults to `false`. Set it to add the canonical top separator and larger spacing when the action region needs a clear boundary from the preceding fields. The root uses an automatic block-start margin, so it can anchor to the bottom of a parent column that has available height.

Action slots accept `ReactNode`, normally public `Button` or `LinkButton` components. Configure `type="submit"`, `onAction`, `href`, `disabled`, `loading`, confirmation and accessible names on those controls. FormActions has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, loading, invalid, empty or error state of its own. Form submission, pending state, validation, server errors and navigation remain application-owned.

## Accessibility, keyboard, internationalisation and routing

- Passing `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` gives the root `role="group"`. Without an accessible name the root remains a layout `div` with no group role.
- Use one clear primary action. Give destructive actions precise labels and route consequential operations through `ConfirmationDialog` when confirmation is warranted.
- FormActions adds no keyboard model. Tab follows DOM order; Enter and Space behaviour belongs to each button. Native form submission belongs to a child `Button type="submit"`.
- Labels are application-owned, translated copy. The wide layout uses logical leading and trailing edges and inherits provider direction.
- Link actions own their `href` and provider router behaviour. FormActions neither selects a route nor intercepts navigation.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not manually rearrange slots at breakpoints, make two actions look primary, assume visual and Tab order match, attach submission to the root, or pass plain click handlers when a child supports semantic `onAction`. Use `ButtonGroup` for freer layout, `FormSection` for titled field regions, and `ConfirmationDialog` for consequential action confirmation.

FormattedDateTime

/>

# FormattedDateTime

`FormattedDateTime` renders a locale-aware human-readable instant in a semantic HTML `<time>` element.

## Import

```tsx
import { FormattedDateTime } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `FormattedDateTime` to display an instant consistently with `BreezeProvider` locale and time zone. Use `DateTimePicker` to edit an instant, `DatePicker` for a date without time, and `RelativeTime` for a deliberately relative phrase. Do not use it to parse user input or display a zone-free wall-clock value.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { FormattedDateTime } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<FormattedDateTime
  value="2026-07-20T14:30:00+01:00"
  options={{ dateStyle: 'medium', timeStyle: 'short' }}
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.DateAndTime} />

## Values, time zones and behaviour

`value` must be a valid ISO 8601 date-time with an explicit offset, such as `'2026-07-20T14:30:00+01:00'` or a `Z` suffix. The original string is preserved in the `<time dateTime>` attribute. Human-readable text is produced with `Intl.DateTimeFormat` and the provider’s BCP 47 locale.

When `BreezeProvider timeZone` is set, that IANA zone is used for display and overrides `options.timeZone`. Without a provider zone, standard `Intl.DateTimeFormatOptions`, including `timeZone`, apply. The display zone never changes the underlying instant. Invalid or offset-free strings can produce incorrect or failing output; validate data before render.

This is a stateless formatter: it has no controlled, loading, disabled, invalid, empty or callback behaviour. Choose an explicit application fallback rather than passing an empty string. It renders no interactive keyboard behaviour.

## Accessibility and internationalisation

- The semantic `<time>` preserves a machine-readable value while assistive technology reads the visible localised text.
- Supply surrounding prose when the date’s meaning is not obvious. Do not rely on numeric order alone.
- Locale controls ordering, names, punctuation and numbering. Provider direction is inherited from the surrounding document.
- Use `options` to make precision intentional; avoid showing seconds or zone names unless they help the task.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass a calendar-only date, offset-free local date-time, JavaScript `Date`, preformatted prose or children. Do not assume changing the display zone changes the instant. Use `FormattedNumber` for numbers, `RelativeTime` for application-calculated relative wording, and `DateTimePicker` for editing.

FormattedList

 */

./src/formatting/FormattedList/FormattedList.mdx
Locale-aware inline joining of ordered text values with Intl.ListFormat conjunction, disjunction, style and punctuation rules from BreezeProvider locale.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './FormattedList.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
