/>

# FormattedList

`FormattedList` joins an ordered set of text values using locale-appropriate punctuation and conjunction or disjunction wording.

## Import

```tsx
import { FormattedList } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `FormattedList` for a short inline phrase such as “PDF, JPG and PNG”. Use semantic `List` for a structural list that users should navigate item by item, or collection components for selection and interaction. Do not use commas or a hard-coded English “and” to join translated values.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { FormattedList } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<FormattedList
  values={['Email', 'telephone', 'post']}
  options={{ type: 'disjunction' }}
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Disjunction} />

## Behaviour, accessibility, and internationalisation

`values` is an ordered array of already translated strings. `Intl.ListFormat` supplies separators and the final conjunction from the provider BCP 47 locale. `options.type` is `'conjunction'` or `'disjunction'`; `options.style` is `'long'`, `'short'` or `'narrow'`. Runtime defaults are conjunction and long style.

An empty array renders an empty `<span>` and one item renders that item without a separator. Decide an application fallback for empty data. This formatter is stateless: it has no controlled, loading, disabled, invalid, callback or keyboard behaviour.

The `<span>` is inline prose, not an HTML list. Assistive technology reads its localised text in context. Keep items concise, preserve meaningful source order, and use a semantic list when item boundaries matter. Direction is inherited and punctuation follows locale.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass React elements, translate the final joined string, split values on commas, or use this as a navigable list. Use `List` for semantic rows, `FormattedNumber` for locale-aware numeric values, and `Typography` to control surrounding prose hierarchy.

FormattedNumber

Formats a domain-neutral number using the Breeze provider locale.

 * `Intl.NumberFormat` options applied using the provider locale.
 */
options?: NumberFormatOptions

/**
 * Ref to the rendered span.
 */
ref?: Ref<HTMLSpanElement>

/**
 * Numeric value to format.
 */
value: number | bigint
Imports

    options={{ maximumFractionDigits: 2, minimumFractionDigits: 2 }}
    value={24862.4} />;

Docs doc ok
./src/formatting/FormattedNumber/FormattedNumber.mdx
Locale-aware number, currency, percentage, unit or notation output for number and bigint values using Intl.NumberFormat and the BreezeProvider locale.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './FormattedNumber.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Locale-aware number, currency, percentage, unit or notation output for number and bigint values using Intl.NumberFormat and the BreezeProvider locale."
/>

# FormattedNumber

`FormattedNumber` renders a numeric value with locale-aware grouping, decimal symbols, signs, currency, units or notation.

## Import

```tsx
import { FormattedNumber } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `FormattedNumber` for display values whose formatting must follow the provider locale. Formatting does not add domain meaning: specify currency, unit or percentage semantics explicitly.

## When not to use

Use `NumberField` for numeric entry, `Meter` or `ProgressBar` for visualised bounded values, and plain text when the string is a stable identifier rather than a number.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { FormattedNumber } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<FormattedNumber
  value={1042.16}
  options={{ style: 'currency', currency: 'GBP' }}
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Currency} />

## Precision and behaviour

`value` accepts a `number` or `bigint`; the component does not validate exceptional numeric values. Native `Intl.NumberFormat` behaviour applies to `NaN`, positive infinity and negative infinity. `options` is the native formatter contract. Important choices include `style`, `currency`, `unit`, `notation`, `minimumFractionDigits`, `maximumFractionDigits`, `signDisplay` and `useGrouping`. Runtime defaults use decimal style and locale-defined precision.

Percentage style formats `1` as 100%, so convert application ratios deliberately. Currency style requires an ISO 4217 `currency` code such as `'GBP'`; do not infer it from locale. Precision options affect display and rounding, not the source value.

This component renders a stateless `<span>` with no input, controlled state, callbacks, loading, invalid, disabled or keyboard behaviour. Choose an application fallback for missing data instead of coercing it to zero.

## Accessibility and internationalisation

Assistive technology reads the localised text in its surrounding context. Include a visible label or nearby prose when the number’s meaning is unclear; do not rely on currency symbols or colour alone. Locale controls digits, grouping, signs, separators and placement. Direction is inherited.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass a preformatted string, infer currency from locale, treat a percentage as an already multiplied value, or use displayed rounding for calculations. Use `NumberField` for editing, `FormattedList` for joined prose and `Meter` for a bounded measurement visualisation.

FormSection

Groups related form controls with a consistent heading and divider rhythm.

 */
action?: ReactNode

/**

 */

/**

 */

/**
 * Adds the canonical outer divider for the selected layout. Defaults to `false`.
 */
divided?: boolean = false

/**
 * Semantic level for the persistent section heading. Defaults to `2`.
 */
headingLevel?: FormSectionHeadingLevel = 2

/**
 * Page split or overlay stacked arrangement. Defaults to `split`.
 */
layout?: FormSectionLayout = split

/**
 * Ref to the rendered section.
 */

/**
 * Persistent section heading.
 */
title: ReactNode

split form section with introduction and related action

const WithAction = () => <FormSection
    action={<Button appearance="text">Add field</Button>}
    description="Information used for workspace notifications."

const StackedAndDivided = () => <FormSection
    description="Information used for workspace notifications."
    divided
    headingLevel={3}
    layout="stacked"
    title="Notification details">(<TextField.Root defaultValue="team@example.com">
      <TextField.Label>Email address</TextField.Label>
      <TextField.Input type="email" />
    </TextField.Root>)</FormSection>;

./src/patterns/FormSection/FormSection.mdx
Accessible titled region for related controls, with generated heading association, split or stacked responsive layout, optional section action and canonical divider rhythm.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './FormSection.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Accessible titled region for related controls, with generated heading association, split or stacked responsive layout, optional section action and canonical divider rhythm."
/>

# FormSection

`FormSection` groups related controls into a named section with a persistent heading, optional explanation and optional section action.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, FormSection, TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `FormSection` to divide a longer page or overlay form into understandable titled regions. It supplies page-level information architecture and layout; child fields retain their own labels, validation and state.

Use `Fieldset` when controls form one semantic question, especially radio buttons or checkboxes. Use `SectionHeader` for a non-form content section and `FormActions` for the form’s final action region. Do not wrap every individual field in FormSection.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, FormSection, TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<FormSection
  action={
    <Button appearance="text" onAction={addContact}>
      Add contact
    </Button>
  }
  description="Details used for service notifications."
  title="Contact details"
>
  <TextField.Root>
    <TextField.Label>Email address</TextField.Label>
    <TextField.Input name="email" type="email" />
  </TextField.Root>
</FormSection>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.WithAction} />

## Anatomy

FormSection is a fixed semantic composition rather than a public compound namespace.

| Region         | Responsibility                                                  |
| -------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Root section   | A native `section` named by its generated heading id.           |
| Header         | Required `title` heading and optional `description`.            |
| Section action | Optional action related to the section, not the entire form.    |
| Fields         | Required `children`, normally labelled Breeze field primitives. |

The generated heading association makes the section discoverable as a named region. `headingLevel` changes semantic hierarchy, not visual prominence. Choose it from the surrounding page outline; levels 2 through 6 are supported and 2 is the default.

## Layout and dividers

| Layout    | Default arrangement                                                                   | `divided` behaviour                                                       |
| --------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `split`   | Header above fields on compact screens; header and fields form two columns from `md`. | Adds a top divider and spacing, except on the first split section.        |
| `stacked` | Header, action and fields form a single vertical composition suited to overlays.      | Adds an outer bottom divider; the stacked header keeps its own separator. |

With an `action`, compact layout places fields before the action visually; from `md`, the action appears above the fields and aligns to the logical end. DOM order does not change, so keyboard and assistive-technology order remain stable.

## Field state and application responsibilities

FormSection owns no field values, validation or submission. Configure controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, required, disabled, invalid, loading, empty and error behaviour on its child controls or surrounding application state. The `action` node owns its callback and state. Do not use the section description as a substitute for each field’s accessible label or associated error.

The section performs no routing. A slotted `LinkButton` or other public link component owns its destination and provider router behaviour.

## Accessibility, internationalisation and styling

- `title` is required and remains visible. Keep heading levels sequential; do not choose a level to obtain a visual size.
- Use `description` for concise section-wide context. Field-specific instructions belong to each field’s Description part.
- Every child control still needs its own label. Use `Fieldset` inside the section when several controls answer one question.
- Translate title, description, action and field copy. Layout uses logical alignment and inherits provider direction.
- `className` is for placement and composition. Do not style generated internal wrappers or depend on internal `data-*` attributes.
- Do not render an empty section; omit it until its fields or meaningful state are available.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not skip heading levels, duplicate the same heading for adjacent regions, use `action` for the whole form’s submit button, put unrelated controls together, or reproduce split layout with application CSS. Use `Fieldset` for one grouped question, `FormActions` for final actions, and `SectionHeader` outside form structure.

/**

 */
columns?: ResponsiveValue<GridColumns> = 1

/**
 * Responsive row and column gap. Defaults to `lg`.
 */

/**
 * Canonical unequal tracks. Excludes equal `columns` while active.
 */
template?: "field-control-action"

Responsive Columns story ok
responsive column counts and gaps
Progressively increases the column count and spacing across Breeze breakpoints while leaving all six items in one stable source order.
const ResponsiveColumns = () => <Grid
    align={args.align}
    aria-label="Grid example"
    columns={{
      base: 1,
      lg: 6,
      md: 3,
    }}
    gap={{
      base: 'sm',
      md: 'md',
    }}>
    <Surface padding="md">One</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Two</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Three</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Four</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Five</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Six</Surface>
</Grid>;

Uses a two-column form-style layout above the small breakpoint and verifies equal tracks plus natural wrapping for the remaining item.
const CanonicalFormColumns = () => <StoryConstraint size="bounded-layout">
    <Grid
        aria-label="Grid example"
        columns={{
          base: 1,
          sm: 2,
        }}
        gap={args.gap}>
        <Surface padding="md">One</Surface>
        <Surface padding="md">Two</Surface>
        <Surface padding="md">Three</Surface>
    </Grid>
</StoryConstraint>;

./src/primitives/Grid/Grid.mdx
A bounded responsive grid with stable source order and canonical track templates.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Grid.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A bounded responsive grid with stable source order and canonical track templates."
/>

# Grid

`Grid` arranges related content in equal responsive columns or the canonical field-control-action track template.

## Import

```tsx
import { Grid } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Grid` when content needs aligned rows and columns or a column count that changes at Breeze breakpoints. Use `Stack` for a single vertical flow, `Inline` for a wrapping row, and `Table` for genuinely tabular data.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Grid, Surface } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SummaryGrid() {
  return (
    <Grid columns={{ base: 1, md: 3 }} gap={{ base: 'md', md: 'lg' }}>
      <Surface>First summary</Surface>
      <Surface>Second summary</Surface>
      <Surface>Third summary</Surface>
    </Grid>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ResponsiveColumns} />

## Columns, templates, and spacing

`columns` accepts `1`, `2`, `3`, `4`, `6`, or `12`, directly or as `{ base, sm?, md?, lg? }`. Breakpoints are mobile-first at 681 px, 901 px, and 1181 px. `gap` uses the Breeze spacing values `none`, `xs`, `sm`, `control`, `compact`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl`, and `page` in the same scalar or responsive form.

Use `template="field-control-action"` only for the established three-track form row: a flexible field, an automatic-width control, and a square final action. While a template is active, `columns` is ignored.

```tsx
<Grid template="field-control-action" align="end" gap="sm">
  <label>
    Reference <input />
  </label>
  <span>Status</span>
  <button type="button" aria-label="Remove">
    ×
  </button>
</Grid>
```

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and direction

Native `div` attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported; inline `style` is excluded. `Grid` has no interactive, controlled, loading, invalid, empty, or error state.

`Grid` is a CSS layout, not an ARIA grid, and supplies no role or keyboard interaction. Keep DOM order meaningful because responsive columns do not reorder content. Direction-aware CSS handles inline flow; avoid visual reordering that would diverge from reading and focus order.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `Grid` for data requiring row/column headers, sorting, or selection; use `Table` or `GridList`.
- Do not pass both a meaningful `columns` value and `template`; the template wins.
- Do not assume every item spans one conceptual column in a 12-column layout; Breeze exposes no span API.

## Related components

Use `Container` around page grids, `Stack` or `Inline` for one-dimensional flow, and `Surface` or `Card` for the items.

IconButton

 */

labelled icon-button activation
Activates an icon-only add action and verifies that its semantic callback is invoked once through the button's required accessible name.
const Activation = () => <IconButton aria-label="Add item" onAction={fn()}><AddIcon /></IconButton>;

Compares canonical square sizes and uses solid danger emphasis for a destructive action without changing the decorative status of its icon.
const SizesAndEmphasis = () => (
  <Inline gap="compact" wrap={false}>
    <IconButton aria-label="Settings small" size="sm">
      <SettingsIcon />
    </IconButton>
    <IconButton aria-label="Settings">
      <SettingsIcon />
    </IconButton>
    <IconButton
      aria-label="Delete"
      appearance="solid"
      size="lg"
      variant="danger"
    >
      <DeleteIcon />
    </IconButton>
  </Inline>
);
States story ok

Compares disabled and loading icon actions so unavailable and in-progress behavior remain accessible without replacing the button's name.
const States = () => (
  <Inline align="stretch" gap="compact" wrap={false}>
    <IconButton aria-label="Disabled settings" disabled>
      <SettingsIcon />
    </IconButton>
    <IconButton aria-label="Loading settings" loading>
      <SettingsIcon />
    </IconButton>
  </Inline>
);

Native Form Behaviors story ok
safe native button and submit behavior
Demonstrates the deliberately bounded native button and submit types inside a form while leaving form submission behavior application-owned.
const NativeFormBehaviors = () => (
  <form onSubmit={(event) => event.preventDefault()}>
    <Stack gap="compact">
      <TextField.Root defaultValue="Initial value">
        <TextField.Label>Example value</TextField.Label>
        <TextField.Input />
      </TextField.Root>
      <Inline gap="compact">
        <IconButton aria-label="Button" type="button">
          <SettingsIcon />
        </IconButton>
        <IconButton aria-label="Submit" type="submit">
          <AddIcon />
        </IconButton>
      </Inline>
    </Stack>
  </form>
);

./src/primitives/IconButton/IconButton.mdx
A compact icon-only action with a mandatory accessible label.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './IconButton.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A compact icon-only action with a mandatory accessible label."
/>

# IconButton

`IconButton` performs an application action in a square icon-only control whose required `aria-label` supplies its accessible name.

## Import

```tsx
import { IconButton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { SettingsIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use `IconButton` for a familiar, repeated action where space is constrained and a text label would add noise. Use `Button` when visible text improves recognition, `Link` for navigation, or `Tooltip` to provide supplementary explanation without replacing the accessible name.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { IconButton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { SettingsIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function SettingsButton() {
  return (
    <IconButton aria-label="Open settings" onAction={() => openSettings()}>
      <SettingsIcon />
    </IconButton>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Activation} />

## Label, icon, and emphasis

`aria-label` is required and should name the action, not describe the glyph: use “Delete item”, not “Bin icon”. The child icon is always hidden from assistive technology because the button owns the name. Keep one clear icon as the child.

Appearances are `solid`, `subtle`, `outline`, and `ghost`; variants are `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, and `dark`; square sizes are `sm`, `md`, and `lg`. The defaults deliberately produce a medium, secondary ghost action.

## Activation, forms, and state

`onAction` fires once for pointer, touch, or keyboard activation. `type` is limited to `button` and `submit` and defaults to `button`. `disabled` prevents interaction. `loading` prevents activation while preserving focusability, sets `aria-busy`, retains the accessible label, and temporarily displays a decorative spinner. Error reporting belongs to the surrounding workflow; there is no invalid, read-only, controlled, or empty state.

<Canvas of={Stories.States} />

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and keyboard behaviour

Relevant native button attributes such as `name`, `value`, `form`, `autoFocus`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported. Breeze owns native click, disabled, pressed, busy, type, class, and style behaviour.

`Enter` and `Space` activate enabled icon buttons, and keyboard focus has a visible indicator. Do not depend on a tooltip as the only name, and do not repeat the icon name in the label. Translate `aria-label` with the rest of the interface. Verify that the chosen icon remains understandable in both directions; directional icon mirroring is icon-specific.

## Common mistakes

- Do not omit `aria-label` or use a vague label such as “Click here”.
- Do not place visible text inside an `IconButton`; use `Button`.
- Do not use an icon-only control for an unfamiliar or high-consequence action without sufficient nearby context.
- Do not add `aria-label` to the child icon; it is deliberately decorative here.

## Related components

Use `Button` for visible labels, `ToggleButton` for persistent on/off state, `Tooltip` for supplementary help, and the curated Breeze icon catalogue for supported icons.

IconTile

      <CalendarIcon />

);

  <Inline gap="compact" wrap={false}>
    <IconTile
      bordered={false}
      data-testid="income-marker"
      shape="circle"
      size="sm"
      variant="success"
    >
      <ArrowRightIcon />
    </IconTile>
    <IconTile
      bordered={false}
      data-testid="expense-marker"
      shape="circle"
      size="sm"
      variant="danger"
    >
      <ArrowLeftIcon />
    </IconTile>
    <IconTile
      bordered={false}
      data-testid="neutral-marker"
      shape="circle"
      size="sm"
      variant="neutral"
    >
      <ArrowLeftIcon />
    </IconTile>
    <IconTile
      bordered={false}
      data-testid="status-code"
      decorative={false}
      size="lg"
    >
      404
    </IconTile>
  </Inline>
);

./src/primitives/IconTile/IconTile.mdx
Non-interactive semantic presentation tile for curated icons and short visual markers.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './IconTile.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Non-interactive semantic presentation tile for curated icons and short visual markers."
/>

# IconTile

`IconTile` places an icon or short marker in canonical geometry with constrained semantic colour emphasis.

## Import

```tsx
import { IconTile } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { CalendarIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

- Use as a supporting visual marker in a header, state panel, summary, or record.
- Use `variant` to reinforce a meaning already expressed in text.
- Use `decorative={false}` only when the marker contributes information not available in nearby text.

## When not to use

- Use `IconButton` when the icon activates an action; IconTile is not interactive.
- Use `Avatar` when the content represents a named person or entity.
- Use `Badge` for compact visible status text.
- Use a curated icon directly when no enclosing geometry or semantic surface is needed.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { IconTile, Inline, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { CalendarIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function ScheduleSummary() {
  return (
    <Inline align="center" gap="sm" wrap={false}>
      <IconTile variant="info">
        <CalendarIcon />
      </IconTile>
      <Typography>Next review date</Typography>
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SemanticTiles} />

## Accessibility mode

`decorative` defaults to `true`, adding `aria-hidden="true"` to the whole tile. This is correct when adjacent visible text supplies the meaning. Any accessible label on a nested icon is also hidden by the tile.

When the marker is the only source of information, expose it and name the meaningful child:

```tsx
import { IconTile } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { WarningIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function WarningMarker() {
  return (
    <IconTile decorative={false} variant="warning">
      <WarningIcon aria-label="Needs attention" />
    </IconTile>
  );
}
```

For visible short text such as a status code, `decorative={false}` exposes that text naturally. Do not expose both a marker and adjacent text with the same wording if that creates repetitive announcements.

## Variants, geometry, and size

Variants are `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, `dark`, and `neutral`. They set the border, background, and foreground together. Semantic variants reinforce, but never replace, visible status language.

`square` is the default geometry; `circle` is useful for directional or compact status markers. Sizes are `sm` (36 pixels), `md` (40 pixels, the default), and `lg` (56 pixels). Child icons sized in `em` inherit the tile's text scale.

`bordered={false}` removes the ordinary semantic border. Breeze may still expose a boundary in forced-colour mode so the marker remains perceivable.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## State and behaviour

Relevant native span attributes such as `id`, `aria-label` when exposed, `data-*`, and relationship attributes are forwarded. `aria-hidden` is owned by `decorative`; arbitrary inline `style` is not a public styling contract.

IconTile has no keyboard behaviour and no controlled, uncontrolled, disabled, loading, invalid, error, empty, or read-only state. It must not receive action semantics or click handling. State belongs to the surrounding component; the tile only presents a marker.

The geometry is direction-neutral. Directional icons retain their named glyph orientation; select the icon that describes the intended concept in the current interface. Child text follows provider direction but is not translated or formatted.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not use colour or an icon alone to communicate critical state.
- Do not set `decorative={false}` without accessible meaning in the child or wrapper.
- Do not give a nested icon an `aria-label` while leaving the tile decorative.
- Do not put long text, controls, or multiple competing icons inside the tile.
- Use typed props rather than overriding canonical geometry or semantic colours with classes.

## Related components

- Use the curated `icons` subpath for the marker glyph.
- Use `IconButton` for an icon-only action.
- Use `Avatar` for identity and `Badge` for status text.
- Use `StatePanel` when the marker belongs to a complete empty, loading, or error composition.

Inline

layout-inline · ./src/primitives/Inline/Inline.stories.tsx
Arranges related content along the inline axis with bounded alignment, distribution, responsive spacing, and wrapping controls.
Prop types (react-docgen-typescript) 4 prop types 
Component: src/primitives/Inline/Inline.tsx::Inline
Props:
/**
 * Cross-axis alignment. Defaults to `center`.
 */
align?: "baseline" | "center" | "end" | "start" | "stretch"

/**
 * Responsive space between children. Defaults to `md`.
 */
gap?: ResponsiveSpace = md

/**
 * Main-axis distribution. Defaults to `start`.
 */
justify?: "around" | "between" | "center" | "end" | "evenly" | "start"

/**
 * Whether children may flow onto additional rows. Defaults to `true`.
 */
wrap?: boolean

Wrapping story ok
wrapping inline layout for related content
Arranges four related surfaces in a wrapping horizontal flow with controlled alignment, distribution, and responsive spacing props.
const Wrapping = () => <Inline align="center" aria-label="Inline example" gap="md" justify="start" wrap>
    <Surface padding="md">Alpha</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Bravo</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Charlie</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Delta</Surface>

Distributed story ok
non-wrapping space-between distribution
Distributes safely bounded content across the available inline axis and disables wrapping when the host width is known to be sufficient.
const Distributed = () => <Inline
    align="center"
    aria-label="Inline example"
    gap="md"
    justify="between"
    wrap={false}>
    <Surface padding="md">Alpha</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Bravo</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Charlie</Surface>
    <Surface padding="md">Delta</Surface>
</Inline>;
Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/Inline/Inline.mdx
A horizontal layout with responsive spacing, alignment, distribution, and wrapping.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Inline.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A horizontal layout with responsive spacing, alignment, distribution, and wrapping."
/>

# Inline

`Inline` arranges related children along the inline axis and wraps them by default.

## Import

```tsx
import { Inline } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Inline` for button rows, metadata, badges, and controls that form one horizontal group. Use `Toolbar` when related controls need toolbar semantics and arrow-key navigation, `Stack` for vertical flow, or `Grid` for aligned columns.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, Inline } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function Actions() {
  return (
    <Inline gap="compact" justify="end">
      <Button appearance="outline">Cancel</Button>
      <Button>Save changes</Button>
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Wrapping} />

## Layout and responsive spacing

`align` controls the cross-axis and includes `baseline` for text with different sizes. `justify` distributes items along the row. Leave `wrap` enabled unless the composition is guaranteed to fit at every supported viewport and zoom level.

`gap` accepts `none`, `xs`, `sm`, `control`, `compact`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl`, or `page`, directly or as `{ base, sm?, md?, lg? }`; breakpoints start at 681 px, 901 px, and 1181 px.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and direction

Relevant native `div` attributes are supported, including `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*`; inline `style` is excluded. There are no controlled, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error states.

`Inline` adds no role, label, focus management, or keyboard behaviour. Wrapping changes visual rows without changing reading or tab order. In right-to-left direction, the browser lays out the inline axis accordingly; do not hard-code left/right spacing or reverse DOM order.

## Common mistakes

- Do not set `wrap={false}` for user-generated labels or action sets that may grow after translation.
- Do not use spacing characters or per-child margins instead of `gap`.
- Do not use `Inline` as a substitute for `Toolbar` semantics.

## Related components

Use `Stack` for vertical flow, `Grid` for responsive columns, `Toolbar` for keyboard-navigable actions, and `ButtonGroup` for a canonical grouped-action pattern.

Link

 */

    <Link href="/primary">Primary</Link>
    <Link href="/muted" variant="muted">
      Muted
    </Link>

    </Link>
    <Link disabled href="/disabled">

    </Link>
  </Inline>
);

./src/primitives/Link/Link.mdx
Router-neutral inline navigation that preserves native link behaviour.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Link.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Router-neutral inline navigation that preserves native link behaviour."
/>

# Link

`Link` navigates to a destination with a real `href`, using the optional Breeze router adapter for eligible local URLs and native browser behaviour everywhere else.

## Import

```tsx
import { Link } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

- Use for inline navigation to another route, page, document, or location.
- Use a local `/path` with the provider router when the application supports client-side routing.
- Use external URLs, `download`, or `target` when native browser navigation is intended.

## When not to use

- Use `Button` for an action that changes state without navigating.
- Use `LinkButton` when navigation needs button-like visual prominence.
- Use `SkipLink` for a keyboard-visible bypass link to main content.
- Do not use a clickable `Typography` or an anchor without an `href` as a substitute.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Link, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function HelpText() {
  return (
    <Typography>
      Read the <Link href="/guidance">usage guidance</Link> before continuing.
    </Typography>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Internal} />

## Routing behaviour

With a `BreezeProvider` router, a local URL beginning with a single `/` uses `router.navigate(href)` for an unmodified primary activation. The link still renders a real `href`, so copying the address, opening a context menu, and browser fallbacks continue to work.

The browser handles navigation when the URL is external or protocol-relative, `download` is present, `target` is not `_self`, the pointer activation is not the primary button, or Alt, Control, Meta, or Shift is held.

```tsx
import {
  BreezeProvider,
  Link,
  type BreezeRouterAdapter,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

const router: BreezeRouterAdapter = {
  navigate: (href) => window.history.pushState(null, '', href),
};

export function Navigation() {
  return (
    <BreezeProvider locale="en-GB" router={router}>
      <Link href="/preferences">Preferences</Link>
    </BreezeProvider>
  );
}
```

## Variants and states

`primary` is the default navigation colour. Use `muted` for deliberately lower-emphasis supporting navigation, `danger` when following the link leads towards a destructive flow, and `inverse` on dark surfaces. Variant conveys visual meaning; the link text must still explain the destination.

`disabled` prevents navigation and semantic activation and renders non-link content. Prefer removing unavailable navigation when that does not hide useful context. If a disabled link must remain visible, explain why it is unavailable nearby.

`onAction` runs after semantic activation and receives no browser event. Put navigation in `href` or the provider adapter, not in `onAction`. Use the callback for application-side observation only and ensure repeated activation is safe.

## External and download links

```tsx
import { Link } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { ExternalLinkIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function ExternalGuidanceLink() {
  return (
    <Link href="https://example.com/guidance" rel="noreferrer" target="_blank">
      External guidance <ExternalLinkIcon />
    </Link>
  );
}
```

Visible wording should make an unexpected new browsing context or download clear. An adjacent icon is decorative by default and does not replace that wording. Supply an appropriate `rel` when using `_blank` according to application security policy.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and keyboard interaction

Relevant native anchor attributes such as `aria-*`, `data-*`, `id`, `hreflang`, `referrerPolicy`, `rel`, and `type` are supported. `onClick`, `style`, and direct React Aria props are not part of the public interaction or styling API.

Use concise text that describes the destination out of context. Avoid “click here”. The native link participates in the tab order, activates with Enter, exposes its destination, and retains browser focus indication. A disabled Link is not a link and is not keyboard focusable.

Do not rely on colour alone to distinguish the link; Breeze includes an underline. When using `variant="inverse"`, provide a sufficiently dark background. The provider direction and locale apply to its text, but URLs and child content are not translated by Link.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not call a router from `onAction`; configure `BreezeProvider.router` and keep the real destination in `href`.
- Do not use `disabled` to represent a loading action; navigation links do not own loading state.
- Do not omit visible context for downloads or links that open a new browsing context.
- Links do not support controlled or uncontrolled value state, invalid state, or read-only state.
- Application routers must update application state after `navigate`; Breeze only invokes the adapter.

## Related components

- Use `LinkButton` for prominent navigation styled as an action control.
- Use `Button` for non-navigation actions.
- Use `Breadcrumbs`, `NavigationList`, or `Tabs` for structured navigation sets.
- Configure local navigation through `BreezeProvider`.

LinkButton

./src/primitives/LinkButton/LinkButton.mdx
Router-neutral navigation presented with canonical button emphasis.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './LinkButton.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Router-neutral navigation presented with canonical button emphasis."
/>

# LinkButton

`LinkButton` navigates to a destination while using the visual hierarchy of a button.

## Import

```tsx
import { LinkButton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `LinkButton` when navigation is the outcome but the destination must be prominent, such as “Continue” or “Create item”. Use `Button` for application actions and form submission, and `Link` for inline or lower-emphasis navigation.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { LinkButton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ContinueLink() {
  return <LinkButton href="/next-step">Continue</LinkButton>;
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Internal} />

## Routing and native navigation

Every `LinkButton` requires a real `href`, preserving native link semantics and fallback navigation. When `BreezeProvider` has a router adapter, an unmodified primary click on a root-relative URL such as `/items` calls `router.navigate(href)`. Protocol-relative URLs, external URLs, downloads, non-`_self` targets, and clicks with modifier keys retain native browser behaviour.

```tsx
<LinkButton href="/reports/latest" download>
  Download report
</LinkButton>
```

## Emphasis and state

Appearances are `solid`, `subtle`, `outline`, and `ghost`; semantic variants are `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, and `dark`; sizes are `sm`, `md`, and `lg`. Choose a danger variant only when the destination itself represents a destructive or high-risk path, not for an action that should be a `Button`.

`disabled` prevents navigation, removes the `href`, and exposes `aria-disabled` while retaining the link’s accessible identity. Prefer removing unavailable navigation or explaining why it is unavailable. `LinkButton` has no loading, callback, controlled, read-only, invalid, error, or empty state.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalisation

Relevant native anchor attributes such as `rel`, `hreflang`, `referrerPolicy`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported. Breeze owns click interception, `href`, `download`, `className`, and inline styling.

Use destination-oriented text that makes sense out of context. `Enter` follows an enabled link; `Space` does not emulate a button. For a new browsing context, communicate that behaviour and use an appropriate `rel`. Decorative icons must not replace the visible link purpose. Allow translated labels to grow and ensure direction-sensitive icons are chosen or mirrored by the icon implementation where appropriate.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `LinkButton` for mutations, modal triggers, or submit behaviour.
- Do not omit `href` and simulate navigation in an action callback.
- Do not intercept external, download, target, or modified-click behaviour in application code.
- Do not use `disabled` as a long-term substitute for clear navigation rules.

## Related components

Use `Link` for ordinary navigation, `Button` for actions, `IconButton` for icon-only actions, and configure routing through `BreezeProvider`.

Logo

./src/primitives/Logo/Logo.mdx

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Logo.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Canonical accessible Motech Development brand mark in three supported sizes."
/>

# Logo

`Logo` renders the canonical Motech Development mark with a fixed accessible name and Breeze-owned brand colour.

## Import

```tsx
import { Logo } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

- Use when the Motech Development brand mark is required in application chrome or an identity treatment.
- Pair it with application-owned product text when a product name must appear beside the mark.
- Choose the nearest canonical size and let the containing layout provide spacing.

## When not to use

- Use `Avatar` for a person or entity identity; Logo is not a generic image placeholder.
- Use a curated icon for an action, status, or navigation affordance.
- Do not use Logo to represent an application, organisation, or partner that has a different identity.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Inline, Logo, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ProductIdentity() {
  return (
    <Inline align="center" gap="sm" wrap={false}>
      <Logo />
      <Typography as="span" level="label">
        Workspace
      </Typography>
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Sizes} />

## Anatomy and sizing

Logo is one non-interactive `span` with `role="img"` and the accessible name “Motech Development”. Its inline SVG is hidden from assistive technology so the mark is announced once.

The `sm`, `md`, and `lg` sizes set canonical surrounding text scales of 1rem, 1.25rem, and 1.5rem. The mark itself is sized in `em`, so it remains aligned with adjacent text. `md` is the default.

The mark uses the Breeze `--breeze-brand-mark` token. On an inverse Breeze surface that token supplies the approved contrasting treatment; applications should not recolour the internal SVG.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and behaviour

Relevant native span attributes such as `id`, `data-*`, and relationship attributes are forwarded. `children`, `aria-label`, `style`, and colour overrides are intentionally excluded: the geometry, accessible brand name, and colour treatment are canonical.

Logo has no keyboard interaction and no controlled, uncontrolled, disabled, loading, invalid, or read-only state. When Logo is the only content of a link, its fixed accessible name becomes the link name; ensure “Motech Development” accurately describes that destination. If a product-home link needs a different name, include visible or visually hidden product text in the link.

The mark is direction-neutral and its accessible name is not translated by `BreezeProvider`. Product text beside it follows the provider locale and direction normally.

## Common mistakes and limitations

- Do not copy the internal SVG or depend on its paths, view box, or classes.
- Do not pass children or replace the accessible name.
- Do not stretch the mark with arbitrary width and height styles.
- Do not use Logo as a clickable element; wrap it in `Link` when it is genuinely a destination.
- The component provides only the Motech Development mark, not product lock-ups or application-owned wording.

## Related components

- Use `Avatar` for people and entity markers.
- Use `IconTile` to place an icon or short marker in semantic geometry.
- Use `Link` when the brand mark leads to a destination.
- Use `ApplicationShell` for the surrounding application identity and navigation structure.

 */

 */
valueText?: ReactNode

/**
 * Bootstrap-aligned semantic colour. Defaults to `primary`.
 */
variant?: VisualVariant = primary

complete semantic meter variant comparison
Compares every supported semantic fill colour at the same value so a measurement's meaning can be chosen independently from its magnitude.

  <Stack gap="md">
    <Meter label="Primary measurement" value={60} variant="primary" />
    <Meter label="Secondary measurement" value={60} variant="secondary" />
    <Meter label="Success measurement" value={60} variant="success" />
    <Meter label="Danger measurement" value={60} variant="danger" />
    <Meter label="Warning measurement" value={60} variant="warning" />
    <Meter label="Information measurement" value={60} variant="info" />
    <Meter label="Light measurement" value={60} variant="light" />
    <Meter label="Dark measurement" value={60} variant="dark" />
  </Stack>
);

./src/primitives/Meter/Meter.mdx
Labelled scalar measurement within a known finite range, with clamped visual and accessible output.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Meter.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled scalar measurement within a known finite range, with clamped visual and accessible output."
/>

# Meter

`Meter` displays one scalar measurement within a known range.

```tsx
import { Meter } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use a meter for a stable quantity such as storage use, signal quality, or capacity. Use [ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs) for operation completion, [MetricCard](?path=/docs/patterns-data-metriccard--docs) for a prominent standalone metric, and [Slider](?path=/docs/selection-slider--docs) when the user changes the value.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Meter } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function StorageUsage() {
  return (
    <Meter
      label="Storage used"
      value={72}
      valueText="72 gigabytes used"
      variant="warning"
    />
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Usage} />

## Range and value behaviour

`minimum` defaults to `0` and `maximum` to `100`. Both bounds must be finite and `maximum` must be greater than or equal to `minimum`; invalid bounds throw `RangeError`. Finite values outside the range are clamped consistently for visual width, visible copy, and accessible value. Use `valueText` to provide translated units or qualitative meaning.

Meter is always determinate and read-only. It has no callback, keyboard interaction, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error state.

## Variants and accessibility

`variant` defaults to `primary` and accepts `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, or `dark`. Choose a semantic colour only when it represents the measurement's meaning, and never depend on colour alone. The required visible `label` names the `meter`. Localise `label` and `valueText`; numeric formatting is application-owned. Logical layout follows the inherited direction.

<Canvas of={Stories.CustomRange} />

`Meter` supports relevant native `div` attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `children`, `role`, and inline `style` are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `Meter` as an input or progress indicator.
- Do not pass non-finite bounds or reverse the range.
- Do not rely on an unlocalised raw number when units matter.
- Do not communicate warning or danger only through `variant`.

## Related components

[ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs), [Slider](?path=/docs/selection-slider--docs), and [MetricCard](?path=/docs/patterns-data-metriccard--docs).

MetricCard

 */

    aria-hidden
    density="spacious"
    label={<Skeleton className="h-5 w-32" tone="inverse" />}
    tone="inverse"
    value={<Skeleton className="h-9 w-48" tone="inverse" />} />;

Uses the subtle surface when a metric belongs inside a light content region and does not need the visual weight of an inverse shell treatment.
const Subtle = () => <MetricCard label="Open items" tone="subtle" value={<FormattedNumber value={12} />} />;

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './MetricCard.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled metric article presenting one prominent application-formatted value with optional context, surface tone, and content rhythm."
/>

# MetricCard

`MetricCard` presents one prominent, labelled metric without calculating, formatting, or fetching it.

## Import

```tsx
import { FormattedNumber, MetricCard } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it for a standalone key performance indicator, count, percentage, or other named value that deserves visual prominence.

## When not to use

Do not use it for a broad content summary, a set of equally weighted facts, bounded progress, or an inline value. Prefer `Card` for the broader summary, `DescriptionList` for several label-value facts, `Meter` for a value within a bounded range, and `FormattedNumber` for locale-aware inline output.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { FormattedNumber, MetricCard } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<MetricCard
  description="Updated recently"
  label="Project progress"
  tone="subtle"
  value={<FormattedNumber options={{ style: 'percent' }} value={0.72} />}
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Subtle} />

## Value ownership, tones, and density

`label` and `value` are required React nodes; `description` is optional. Breeze applies tabular numerals to the value region but does not convert units, calculate trends, apply precision, or parse strings. Supply a domain-ready node and use `FormattedNumber`, `FormattedDateTime`, or `RelativeTime` when locale-aware display is appropriate.

| Prop value           | Effect                                                 |
| -------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `tone="default"`     | Canonical light surface and strong label; the default. |
| `tone="subtle"`      | Quieter light surface for supporting page information. |
| `tone="inverse"`     | Dark shell-compatible surface with inverse copy.       |
| `density="regular"`  | Canonical internal content gap; the default.           |
| `density="spacious"` | More space between label, value, and description.      |

Tone and density are scalar presentation choices, not responsive objects. Choose tone from the surrounding surface and hierarchy, not from the numeric value's sign. Put semantic change or risk in explicit text rather than colour alone.

## Loading, empty, and error states

The application owns fetching and status. For a predictable loading layout, pass tone-compatible `Skeleton` nodes for label and value and hide the placeholder article with `aria-hidden`; announce loading in a separate status region. Do not pass an empty string as a finished value. Use an explicit localised placeholder when “not available” is meaningful, and use `StatePanel` or `Alert` when the entire metric cannot be loaded.

MetricCard has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, invalid, loading, or error prop and no callbacks.

## Accessibility and internationalisation

The semantic root is an `article` named by the visible label through a generated `aria-labelledby` relationship. Make labels concise and unique enough to distinguish adjacent metric articles. Include units in the formatted value or visible description, and do not rely on a visual abbreviation that a screen reader cannot interpret.

The card has no keyboard behaviour; interactive content generally does not belong in its value. Format numbers with the provider locale and dates/times with an explicit application time-zone policy. Translate label, description, and fallback copy. Logical layout supports both text directions, while numeral shaping follows the chosen locale and formatter options.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not perform calculations in presentation copy, omit units, communicate good/bad state only through tone, put a form control inside the value, or render several unrelated measures in one card. Related components: [FormattedNumber](?path=/docs/formatting-formattednumber--docs), [Card](?path=/docs/data-display-card--docs), [DescriptionList](?path=/docs/data-display-descriptionlist--docs), [Meter](?path=/docs/feedback-meter--docs), and [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs).

NavigationSection

 */

Visible section heading and labelled navigation landmark for one related group of application destinations.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './NavigationSection.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Visible section heading and labelled navigation landmark for one related group of application destinations."
/>

# NavigationSection

`NavigationSection` groups related destinations under a persistent visible heading that also names the navigation landmark.

## Import

```tsx
import {
  NavigationList,
  NavigationSection,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it when a navigation area contains several meaningful groups, such as project and account destinations. Use `NavigationList.Root` alone for one already-labelled destination set, `Breadcrumbs` for hierarchy, `Tabs` for peer content views, or `SectionHeader` for a non-navigation content section.

## Basic example

```tsx
import {
  NavigationList,
  NavigationSection,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<NavigationSection title="Projects">
  <NavigationList.Root aria-label="Project destinations">
    <NavigationList.Item current href="/overview" id="overview">
      Overview
    </NavigationList.Item>
    <NavigationList.Item href="/projects" id="projects">
      All projects
    </NavigationList.Item>
  </NavigationList.Root>
</NavigationSection>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Destinations} />

## Structure, state, and routing

The pattern renders a `nav` landmark named by its visible fixed-level `h2`, followed by `children`. It adds no selection model: the application derives each destination's `current` state from its router. `NavigationList.Item` local links use the `BreezeProvider` router adapter when configured; external destinations and native browser link behaviour remain native.

The section has no orientation, density, variant, disabled, loading, invalid, empty, or error state. Configure layout and item state on the child navigation primitive. When there are no destinations, omit the entire section instead of leaving an empty landmark; when loading fails, present feedback outside the navigation.

## Accessibility and internationalisation

`title` must be concise, visible, and distinct from other navigation landmark names. Translate the title and destination labels. If `children` contains another navigation landmark such as `NavigationList.Root`, give it a more specific accessible label so landmarks remain distinguishable.

The pattern itself adds no keyboard behaviour. Links in its children retain standard `Tab`, `Enter`, modified-click, and context-menu behaviour. Item callbacks use the semantics of the composed navigation component. Logical spacing follows provider direction; destination order remains application-owned in both left-to-right and right-to-left layouts.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use a section heading as a substitute for marking the current route, repeat the same title for every landmark, place commands among destinations, or render unrelated groups under one title. Related components: [NavigationList](?path=/docs/navigation-navigationlist--docs), [ApplicationShell](?path=/docs/patterns-application-shell-applicationshell--docs), [Breadcrumbs](?path=/docs/navigation-breadcrumbs--docs), and [SectionHeader](?path=/docs/patterns-page-structure-sectionheader--docs).

 */

/**

 */

/**
 * Unique page heading.
 */
title: ReactNode

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './PageHeader.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Responsive page-level header combining the unique page heading, concise context, optional back destination, and application actions."
/>

# PageHeader

`PageHeader` establishes page hierarchy with one `h1`, optional supporting context, back navigation, and responsive page actions.

## Import

```tsx
import {
  Button,
  ButtonGroup,
  Link,
  PageHeader,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it once near the start of a page's main content. Use `SectionHeader` for an `h2` within the page, `Typography` for an ordinary heading without page-header composition, and `Breadcrumbs` instead of a single back destination when the full hierarchy matters.

## Basic example

```tsx
import {
  Button,
  ButtonGroup,
  Link,
  PageHeader,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<PageHeader
  actions={
    <ButtonGroup
      aria-label="Project actions"
      orientation={{ base: 'verticalReverse', sm: 'horizontal' }}
    >
      <Button appearance="outline">View archived</Button>
      <Button>Create project</Button>
    </ButtonGroup>
  }
  back={<Link href="/projects">Back to projects</Link>}
  description="Review and manage active projects."
  title="Projects"
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ActionsAndBack} />

## Anatomy and responsive behaviour

The semantic `header` contains a title column and an optional actions region. `back` appears before the title and remains content-sized. `title` is always the page's `h1`; `description` sits directly beneath it. Actions stack below the title area until the wide `lg` two-column layout, stretch on compact screens, become content-sized from `sm`, and align to the end at `lg`.

The slot does not reorder action DOM nodes. Use `ButtonGroup` responsive orientation when primary action priority should change visually while DOM and keyboard order remain stable. Keep actions short and directly related to the page.

## Accessibility, state, and routing

Use one `PageHeader` per page and avoid another `h1` unless the surrounding document genuinely requires it. The generated `aria-labelledby` names the header from its visible title. Supply a real `Link` in `back`; local URLs use the provider router adapter, while external and native link behaviours stay native. Do not make a back control call `history.back()` when a stable parent destination is available.

The pattern itself is non-interactive and adds no keyboard behaviour. Back links and action controls retain their documented focus and activation behaviour. It has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, loading, invalid, empty, or error state: express those states in the slotted actions or page content. A loading action should use `Button loading`; a page-level failure usually belongs in `StatePanel` or `Alert`, not in `description` alone.

Translate all slot content. Layout uses logical alignment and works in both directions; application-authored arrows should either use direction-aware copy/icons or be omitted in favour of a meaningful link label.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use it for every card or section, place unrelated global controls in `actions`, put a button where `back` should be a destination, or rely on a long description for essential error feedback. Related components: [SectionHeader](?path=/docs/patterns-page-structure-sectionheader--docs), [ButtonGroup](?path=/docs/patterns-actions-buttongroup--docs), [Breadcrumbs](?path=/docs/navigation-breadcrumbs--docs), and [ApplicationShell](?path=/docs/patterns-application-shell-applicationshell--docs).

Pagination

 */

/**
 * Current one-based page.
 */
currentPage: number

/**
 * Prevents all page changes. Defaults to `false`.
 */
disabled?: boolean = false

/**
 * Called with the requested one-based page.
 */
onChange: (page: number) => void

/**
 * Total number of pages.
 */
pageCount: number

/**
 * Ref to the rendered navigation landmark.
 */

const ManyPages = () => <Pagination
    aria-label="Example results pages"
    currentPage={4}
    onChange={fn()}
    pageCount={12} />;

    aria-label="Example results pages"
    currentPage={1}
    onChange={fn()}
    pageCount={1} />;
Docs doc ok
./src/patterns/Pagination/Pagination.mdx
Controlled, one-based pagination across a known page count with localised controls, boundaries, and compact ellipses.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Pagination.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Controlled, one-based pagination across a known page count with localised controls, boundaries, and compact ellipses."
/>

# Pagination

`Pagination` requests movement through a known, one-based page range while the application owns data and route state.

## Import

```tsx
import { Pagination } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it when a result set has a stable, known `pageCount` and direct page access is useful.

## When not to use

Do not use it for an unknown total, continuous scrolling, or collections where retaining nearby items is more useful than moving between discrete pages. Prefer an application-owned “Load more” action when the total is unknown, or the virtualisation support in `Table`, `GridList`, and `ListBox` for large scrolling collections.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Pagination } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Pagination
  aria-label="Search result pages"
  currentPage={4}
  onChange={(page) => {
    setPage(page);
    loadResults({ page });
  }}
  pageCount={12}
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ManyPages} />

## State, values, and callbacks

Pagination is controlled. `currentPage` and every value passed to `onChange(page)` are one-based positive integers. The callback reports the requested page and receives no DOM event; update route and data state, then pass the resulting `currentPage` back. There is no uncontrolled `defaultCurrentPage` or read-only state.

`pageCount` must be a positive integer. `currentPage` must be an integer from `1` through `pageCount`, inclusive. Invalid values throw `RangeError` during rendering instead of being clamped. Long ranges always keep first, last, current, and adjacent pages available, with non-interactive ellipses for gaps. The current page is a non-interactive element with `aria-current="page"`, so selecting it cannot trigger a redundant request.

`disabled` defaults to `false` and prevents all page-change controls. Previous and next are also disabled at their respective boundaries. Disabling does not change or hide the current page.

## Data, errors, and routing

The application owns fetching, cancellation, caching, URLs, scroll restoration, focus after navigation, loading, empty results, and errors. Keep the previous page visible while a request is pending and set `disabled`, or replace the results with suitable progress feedback. Do not change `currentPage` to an invalid value when a result count shrinks; resolve the valid page in application state first.

Pagination renders buttons, not links, and does not call the router adapter. When every page needs a crawlable or open-in-new-tab URL, compose an application-specific navigation solution from public link primitives.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalisation

The required `aria-label` distinguishes this `nav` landmark from primary navigation. The current page receives the provider's localised `page` label; directional buttons use `previousPage` and `nextPage`. Override those messages through `BreezeProvider` when localising the application.

Controls follow normal button keyboard behaviour: `Tab` visits enabled buttons and `Enter` or `Space` activates them. There is no roving focus or arrow-key page selection. Ellipses are hidden from assistive technology. In right-to-left direction, previous and next arrow icons reverse while callback values remain numerically unchanged.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass zero-based indexes, set `pageCount` to zero for empty results, treat the callback as a DOM event, use pagination for an unknown total, or announce every pending request by replacing the landmark. Related components: [Table](?path=/docs/data-display-table--docs), [GridList](?path=/docs/collections-gridlist--docs), [ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs), and [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs).

PasswordField

 */

/**

 */
required?: boolean = false

/**
 * Canonical input and action size. Defaults to `md`.
 */

/**
 * Current password value.
 * Current immutable password value.
 */
value?: string

./src/patterns/PasswordField/PasswordField.mdx
Labelled password-entry pattern with controlled, uncontrolled or read-only string state, semantic change callbacks, validation anatomy and a provider-localised keyboard-operable visibility action.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './PasswordField.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled password-entry pattern with controlled, uncontrolled or read-only string state, semantic change callbacks, validation anatomy and a provider-localised keyboard-operable visibility action."
/>

# PasswordField

`PasswordField` provides labelled password entry with supporting guidance, validation feedback and a show-or-hide action.

## Import

```tsx
import { PasswordField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `PasswordField` for a current or new password when the canonical label, input, description, error and visibility composition is sufficient.

## When not to use

Use compound `TextField` with `type="password"` when you need native attributes or composition points that PasswordField does not expose. Use a normal `TextField` for non-secret text. PasswordField does not validate password strength, compare fields, generate passwords, store credentials or submit forms.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { PasswordField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { useState } from 'react';

export function NewPasswordField() {
  const [password, setPassword] = useState('');

  return (
    <PasswordField
      autoComplete="new-password"
      description="Use at least 12 characters."
      label="New password"
      name="new-password"
      onChange={setPassword}
      required
      value={password}
    />
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Visibility} />

## Anatomy

PasswordField is a fixed pattern rather than a public compound namespace.

| Region            | Responsibility                                                          |
| ----------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| Label             | Required persistent accessible name for the native input.               |
| Input group       | One visual surface containing the password input and visibility action. |
| Visibility action | Toggles the native input between `type="password"` and `type="text"`.   |
| Description       | Optional persistent requirements or guidance associated with the input. |
| Error             | Optional validation message associated while the field is invalid.      |

Visibility is internal presentation state and always starts hidden. It does not alter the password string. Read-only values may still be revealed; disabled fields disable both editing and the visibility action.

## Value state and callbacks

- **Uncontrolled:** omit `value`; `defaultValue` defaults to an empty string. Optional `onChange` observes edits.
- **Controlled:** provide `value` and `onChange`. The callback receives the complete next password string, never a DOM event.
- **Read-only:** provide `value` with `readOnly`; omit `onChange` and `defaultValue`.

Controlled and uncontrolled props are mutually exclusive. Mutable controlled state requires `onChange`; PasswordField throws if JavaScript bypasses that public TypeScript contract. The application owns password rules, strength calculations, matching, submission, server errors and security handling.

## Sizes, validation and browser behaviour

`size` accepts `'sm'`, `'md'` or `'lg'` and defaults to `'md'`; it sizes both the input and visibility action. Appearance and semantic colour are fixed.

Set `invalid` with meaningful `error` copy to expose invalid state and its associated message. `required` supplies native and accessible required state but does not invent a business rule. `description` remains visible for persistent requirements. `placeholder` is optional example text and never replaces `label`.

Use `autoComplete="current-password"` for an existing credential and `autoComplete="new-password"` for creation or reset. `'off'` is supported but browsers and password managers may choose their own security behaviour. `name` participates in native submission. There is no loading or empty-content mode; use the empty string as the empty value and keep pending submission state on the form action.

## Keyboard, accessibility and internationalisation

- The native input supports ordinary text-entry, selection, copy and paste keyboard behaviour. Tab moves to the visibility button; Enter or Space toggles visibility.
- The visibility icon is decorative. Its required accessible label switches between the provider messages `showPassword` and `hidePassword`; configure those messages for the application locale.
- Keep `label` visible and translate label, guidance and validation copy. Password values are opaque strings and are never translated or reformatted.
- Provider direction is inherited by the field. Do not alter or reverse a password based on writing direction.
- Disabling prevents focus and editing. Read-only preserves focus and copying; explain immutable state when it is not obvious.

PasswordField performs no routing. It exposes no custom class, ref, id, input mode, length constraints, pattern, ARIA attribute pass-through or custom visibility icon; compose `TextField`, `InputGroup` and `IconButton` directly when those deliberate public capabilities are needed.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not put strength instructions only in a placeholder, use `invalid` without useful error copy, log password values in callbacks, disable password-manager support without a concrete reason, or assume the visibility toggle changes value state. Use `TextField` for custom password anatomy and `FormSection` or `FormActions` for surrounding form structure.

ProgressBar

feedback-progressbar · ./src/primitives/ProgressBar/ProgressBar.stories.tsx
Shows measurable or indeterminate operation progress with range semantics.
Prop types (react-docgen-typescript) 8 prop types 
Component: src/primitives/ProgressBar/ProgressBar.tsx::ProgressBar
Props:
/**
 * Excluded or false when progress has a measurable value.
 * Marks progress as ongoing without a measurable value.
 */
indeterminate?: boolean = false

/**
 * Visible application-owned translated progress label.
 */
label: ReactNode

/**
 * Maximum range value. Defaults to `100`.
 */
maximum?: number = 100

/**
 * Minimum range value. Defaults to `0`.
 */
minimum?: number = 0

/**
 * Ref to the rendered progress element.
 */
ref?: Ref<HTMLDivElement>

/**
 * Current progress value within the configured range.
 * Excluded because indeterminate progress has no current value.
 */
value?: number

/**
 * Optional translated value announced instead of the numeric value.
 * Excluded because indeterminate progress has no current value text.
 */
valueText?: string

/**
 * Bootstrap-aligned semantic colour. Defaults to `primary`.
 */
variant?: VisualVariant = primary
Imports 
import { ProgressBar } from "@motech-development/breeze-ui";

    label="Items processed"
    maximum={20}
    minimum={10}
    value={15}
    valueText="15 of 20 items" />;

  <Stack gap="md">
    <ProgressBar
      data-testid="primary-progress"
      label="Primary progress"
      value={60}
      variant="primary"
    />
    <ProgressBar
      data-testid="secondary-progress"
      label="Secondary progress"
      value={60}
      variant="secondary"
    />
    <ProgressBar
      data-testid="success-progress"
      label="Success progress"
      value={60}
      variant="success"
    />
    <ProgressBar
      data-testid="danger-progress"
      label="Danger progress"
      value={60}
      variant="danger"
    />
    <ProgressBar
      data-testid="warning-progress"
      label="Warning progress"
      value={60}
      variant="warning"
    />
    <ProgressBar
      data-testid="info-progress"
      label="Information progress"
      value={60}
      variant="info"
    />
    <ProgressBar
      data-testid="light-progress"
      label="Light progress"
      value={60}
      variant="light"
    />
    <ProgressBar
      data-testid="dark-progress"
      label="Dark progress"
      value={60}
      variant="dark"
    />
  </Stack>
);
Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/ProgressBar/ProgressBar.mdx
Labelled determinate or indeterminate indicator for the progress of an ongoing operation.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ProgressBar.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled determinate or indeterminate indicator for the progress of an ongoing operation."
/>

# ProgressBar

`ProgressBar` communicates how far an operation has progressed, or that its progress cannot yet be measured.

```tsx
import { ProgressBar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it for time-based operation progress such as uploading or processing. Use [Meter](?path=/docs/feedback-meter--docs) for a stable measurement such as storage used, [Spinner](?path=/docs/feedback-spinner--docs) for a compact indeterminate activity mark, and [Stepper](?path=/docs/progression-stepper--docs) for named stages.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { ProgressBar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function UploadProgress({ percentage }: { percentage: number }) {
  return (
    <ProgressBar
      label="Uploading files"
      value={percentage}
      valueText={`${percentage} percent complete`}
    />
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Determinate} />

## Determinate and indeterminate state

Determinate progress requires `value`; `minimum` defaults to `0`, `maximum` to `100`, and `indeterminate` is `false`. Set `indeterminate` to `true` and omit both `value` and `valueText` when no defensible measurement exists. Switch to determinate only when a meaningful value is available.

`valueText` replaces the numeric value announced by assistive technology. Author and translate it when units or range meaning are not obvious. Values are interpreted within the supplied numeric range.

<Canvas of={Stories.Indeterminate} />

## Variants and accessibility

`variant` is `primary` by default and accepts `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, or `dark`. Use semantic colour only as supporting information. The visible translated `label` is required and labels the progressbar. The component has no keyboard interaction, callback, disabled, invalid, empty, or error state; keep operation cancellation in a separate `Button`. Direction is inherited, and formatting application-specific values remains the consumer's responsibility.

`ProgressBar` supports relevant native `div` attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `children`, `role`, and inline `style` are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not use a progress bar for a static scalar measurement.
- Do not invent percentages for an indeterminate operation.
- Do not omit or visually hide the required operation label.
- Do not announce frequent progress changes elsewhere as well.

## Related components

[Meter](?path=/docs/feedback-meter--docs), [Spinner](?path=/docs/feedback-spinner--docs), [Stepper](?path=/docs/progression-stepper--docs), and [Skeleton](?path=/docs/feedback-skeleton--docs).

RelativeTime

const NaturalLanguage = () => <RelativeTime options={{ numeric: 'auto' }} unit="day" value={-1} />;
Docs doc ok
./src/formatting/RelativeTime/RelativeTime.mdx
Locale-aware relative-duration wording from an application-owned signed numeric distance and explicit Intl.RelativeTimeFormat unit; it does not calculate or automatically update time.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './RelativeTime.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Locale-aware relative-duration wording from an application-owned signed numeric distance and explicit Intl.RelativeTimeFormat unit; it does not calculate or automatically update time."
/>

# RelativeTime

`RelativeTime` formats an application-calculated signed distance as locale-aware wording such as “yesterday” or “in 3 days”.

## Import

```tsx
import { RelativeTime } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `RelativeTime` when relative wording helps users compare an event with a clear reference time. Use `FormattedDateTime` when the exact instant matters or the phrase could become ambiguous. Do not use relative wording for legal, audit or deadline data without also making the exact instant available.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { RelativeTime } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<RelativeTime value={-1} unit="day" options={{ numeric: 'auto' }} />;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.NaturalLanguage} />

## Values, units and updating

`value` is a signed distance from the application’s reference time: negative is past, positive is future and zero is current. `unit` is an `Intl.RelativeTimeFormatUnit`: `'year'`, `'quarter'`, `'month'`, `'week'`, `'day'`, `'hour'`, `'minute'` or `'second'` (singular or standard plural aliases where supported). The component does not compare dates, choose a unit, round a duration, run a timer or update itself.

With `options.numeric="always"` the output remains numeric; `"auto"` permits phrases such as “yesterday”. `options.style` supports `'long'`, `'short'` and `'narrow'`. Runtime defaults are `numeric="always"` and long style. Recalculate and rerender at an interval appropriate to the selected unit; avoid minute-by-minute updates for day-level wording.

This stateless `<span>` has no controlled, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, callback or keyboard behaviour.

## Accessibility and internationalisation

Assistive technology reads the visible localised phrase. Provide the exact instant nearby or in accessible supporting text when precision matters; a tooltip alone is not sufficient. Locale controls grammar, pluralisation, numbering and direction. Keep the signed value and unit domain-correct instead of parsing the formatted phrase.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass a timestamp as `value`, expect automatic updates, choose a unit from translated output, or use `numeric="auto"` when exact quantity matters. Use `FormattedDateTime` for a stable instant and `FormattedNumber` for a numeric duration that is not relative to now.

Root
