./src/primitives/TextField/TextField.mdx
Labelled single-line text entry with controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, validation, and native form behaviour.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './TextField.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Labelled single-line text entry with controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, validation, and native form behaviour."
/>

# TextField

`TextField` is the standard compound control for collecting one line of text with a persistent label and associated guidance.

## Import

```tsx
import { TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `TextField` for names, references, email addresses, telephone numbers, URLs, passwords, and other short free-form values. Use `TextArea` for paragraphs, `NumberField` for quantities that need numeric stepping or locale-aware formatting, and `SearchField` for a query that can be submitted or cleared.

Do not use a text field when the valid values form a known set. Prefer `Select` for compact selection, `RadioGroup` when all choices should remain visible, or `ComboBox` when users need to filter a large set.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ContactField() {
  return (
    <TextField.Root>
      <TextField.Label>Email address</TextField.Label>
      <TextField.Input name="email" type="email" autoComplete="email" />
      <TextField.Description>Used for service updates.</TextField.Description>
    </TextField.Root>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AnatomyAndUncontrolled} />

## Anatomy

| Part                    | Purpose                                                                                      |
| ----------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `TextField.Root`        | Owns value, disabled, read-only, required, and invalid state and associates the field parts. |
| `TextField.Label`       | Persistently names the input. It renders a native label.                                     |
| `TextField.Input`       | Renders the native single-line input.                                                        |
| `TextField.Description` | Provides supporting guidance associated with the input.                                      |
| `TextField.Error`       | Provides the accessible validation message when `Root` is invalid.                           |

Keep all parts inside `Root`. A visible `Label` is the preferred accessible name; use `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby` on `Input` only when a visible label is genuinely unsuitable.

## Value and state

Uncontrolled fields use `defaultValue`; controlled fields pair `value` with `onChange`; immutable controlled fields pair `value` with `readOnly` and deliberately omit `onChange`.

```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { TextField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ControlledReferenceField() {
  const [reference, setReference] = useState('BRZ-3');

  return (
    <TextField.Root value={reference} onChange={setReference}>
      <TextField.Label>Reference</TextField.Label>
      <TextField.Input name="reference" />
    </TextField.Root>
  );
}
```

`onChange` receives the complete next string, not a DOM change event. `disabled` prevents focus and editing. `readOnly` keeps the value focusable and available to forms but prevents edits. `required` exposes required semantics; application validation remains responsible for setting `invalid` and rendering a helpful `Error`. An empty value is the empty string. Loading is application-owned; disable the field only when interaction must stop.

## Input types, size, and native attributes

`Input` supports `type="text"`, `"email"`, `"password"`, `"tel"`, and `"url"`, and sizes `sm`, `md` (default), and `lg`. It accepts relevant native input attributes such as `name`, `placeholder`, `autoComplete`, `inputMode`, `pattern`, `minLength`, `maxLength`, and `aria-*`. Put value and state props on `Root`, not `Input`.

## Keyboard and accessibility

- Tab moves focus to and from the native input; normal platform text-editing, selection, clipboard, and undo commands are preserved.
- The label must describe the expected value, not merely repeat the placeholder. Placeholders disappear and are not labels.
- Use `Description` for format guidance before an error occurs. Use `Error` for a specific recovery instruction and set `invalid` on `Root`.
- Keep `required` consistent with the application’s validation rules. Do not encode requiredness through an asterisk alone.
- Use an appropriate `autoComplete` token and `type`; browsers and assistive technology can then offer better input behaviour.

Text values are Unicode strings and support left-to-right and right-to-left content. The component follows the surrounding `dir`; use `dir="auto"` on the input when user-authored content may have an unknown direction.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not pass `value`, `defaultValue`, `disabled`, `readOnly`, `required`, or `onChange` to `Input`; these belong to `Root`.
- Do not switch between controlled and uncontrolled state during the component’s lifetime.
- Do not hide a persistent label to save space; concise labels improve scanning and speech-input use.
- Do not use `invalid` merely to style a warning. It communicates a real validation failure.

## Related components

Use `TextArea` for multiline content, `SearchField` for submitted queries, `NumberField` for numeric values, `PasswordField` for a revealable password pattern, and `InputGroup` to visually combine an input with a unit or related action.

date-and-time-timefield · ./src/primitives/TimeField/TimeField.stories.tsx
Coordinates stable time strings with locale-aware editable segments.
Prop types (react-docgen-typescript) 14 prop types 
Component: src/primitives/TimeField/TimeField.tsx::Root
Props:
/**
 * Compound label, segmented input, description, and error parts.
 */
children: ReactNode

/**
 * Excluded when controlled.
 * Initial uncontrolled time, or `null` when empty.
 */
defaultValue?: string | null

/**
 * Prevents focus and time editing. Defaults to `false`.
 */
disabled?: boolean = false

/**
 * Associates the hidden native time input with an external form.
 */
form?: string

/**
 * Hidden native time input ref used for form integration.
 */
inputRef?: Ref<HTMLInputElement>

/**
 * Exposes invalid state. Defaults to `false`.
 */
invalid?: boolean = false

/**
 * Latest selectable `HH:mm` or `HH:mm:ss` time.
 */
maxValue?: string

/**
 * Earliest selectable `HH:mm` or `HH:mm:ss` time.
 */
minValue?: string

/**
 * Native form field name for the stable time string.
 */
name?: string

/**
 * Called with the next stable time string or `null`.
 * Excluded because read-only times cannot change.
 */
onChange?: ((value: string | null) => void) | ((value: string | null) => void)

/**
 * Mutable state cannot be read-only.
 * Prevents changes to the controlled time.
 * Uncontrolled state cannot be read-only.
 */
readOnly?: boolean = false

/**
 * Ref to the rendered time-field root.
 */
ref?: Ref<HTMLDivElement>

/**
 * Marks the time as required. Defaults to `false`.
 */
required?: boolean = false

/**
 * Current controlled time, or `null` when empty.
 * Current immutable time, or `null` when empty.
 * Excluded when uncontrolled.
 */
value?: string | null
Imports 
import { TimeField } from "@motech-development/breeze-ui";
Segment Keyboard story ok
keyboard editing of an uncontrolled time segment
Starts with an uncontrolled stable time and verifies ArrowUp increments the focused minute segment without exposing internal Time objects.
const SegmentKeyboard = () => (
  <TimeField.Root defaultValue="14:30">
    <TimeField.Label>Time</TimeField.Label>
    <TimeField.Input />
  </TimeField.Root>
);
Seconds Controlled Read Only story ok
controlled minutes and read-only seconds values
Compares an application-controlled minute value with an immutable value that includes seconds, documenting both supported granularities and owners.
const SecondsControlledReadOnly = () => (
  <Stack gap="md">
    <Controlled />
    <TimeField.Root readOnly value="14:30:45">
      <TimeField.Label>Read only seconds</TimeField.Label>
      <TimeField.Input />
    </TimeField.Root>
  </Stack>
);
Boundaries And Form story ok
constrained invalid time field with form integration
Combines native form naming, required invalid feedback, and minimum and maximum times in one constrained office-hours field.
const BoundariesAndForm = () => (
  <TimeField.Root
    form="booking"
    invalid
    maxValue="17:00"
    minValue="09:00"
    name="time"
    required
  >
    <TimeField.Label>Office hours</TimeField.Label>
    <TimeField.Input />
    <TimeField.Description>09:00 to 17:00.</TimeField.Description>
    <TimeField.Error>Select a valid time.</TimeField.Error>
  </TimeField.Root>
);

./src/primitives/TimeField/TimeField.mdx
Locale-aware segmented entry for one wall-clock time, using stable HH:mm or HH:mm:ss values with native form participation and controlled, uncontrolled, or read-only state.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './TimeField.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Locale-aware segmented entry for one wall-clock time, using stable HH:mm or HH:mm:ss values with native form participation and controlled, uncontrolled, or read-only state."
/>

# TimeField

`TimeField` edits a wall-clock time through locale-aware segments without attaching it to a date or time zone.

## Import

```tsx
import { TimeField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `TimeField` for a time of day whose date and zone are separate business concepts. Use `DateTimePicker` for a complete instant with an explicit offset, or `FormattedDateTime` to display rather than edit a date-time. Do not use it for elapsed durations, countdowns or free-form time text.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { TimeField } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<TimeField.Root
  defaultValue="14:30"
  minValue="09:00"
  maxValue="17:00"
  name="startTime"
>
  <TimeField.Label>Start time</TimeField.Label>
  <TimeField.Input />
  <TimeField.Description>Between 09:00 and 17:00.</TimeField.Description>
</TimeField.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SegmentKeyboard} />

## Anatomy

| Part                    | Purpose                                                                     |
| ----------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `TimeField.Root`        | Owns stable time state, boundaries, validation and native form integration. |
| `TimeField.Label`       | Visible accessible name for the segmented input.                            |
| `TimeField.Input`       | Locale-aware editable hour, minute and optional second segments.            |
| `TimeField.Description` | Supporting guidance associated with the field.                              |
| `TimeField.Error`       | Validation message associated with an invalid field.                        |

## Values, precision and state

Use 24-hour stable strings: `'14:30'` for minute precision or `'14:30:45'` for second precision. An empty value is `null`. The visual control may present a locale-appropriate 12- or 24-hour cycle, but callbacks always use the stable 24-hour format. Seconds appear when any of `value`, `defaultValue`, `minValue` or `maxValue` contains three colon-separated segments; keep precision consistent across those props.

Use `defaultValue` for uncontrolled state, `value` with `onChange` for controlled state, or `value` with `readOnly` for immutable state. `onChange` receives the next complete stable time or `null`. `minValue` and `maxValue` are inclusive. `disabled` prevents focus and changes; `readOnly` preserves an inspectable controlled value; `invalid` and `required` expose validation state. Empty and loading behaviour remain application-owned.

When `name` is supplied, the hidden native time input submits the stable value. Use `form` for an external form and `inputRef` only when direct access to that hidden input is necessary.

## Keyboard, accessibility and internationalisation

- Tab enters and leaves the segmented control. Arrow keys move between or increment segments; numeric typing edits the focused segment, and Backspace or Delete clears editable content.
- Always render `Label`. Use `Description` for constraints and `Error` for a correction when `invalid` is true.
- Locale controls visual segment order, hour cycle and spoken labels. Provider direction controls logical layout.
- Do not localise the stored value or infer a time zone from the displayed hour. Daylight-saving rules do not apply because this component has no date or zone.
- Do not communicate required, invalid or disabled state only through appearance.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not pass a JavaScript `Date`, an ISO date-time, a locale-formatted time or an offset such as `+01:00`. Do not mix minute and second precision accidentally. Use `DateField` for a separate calendar date, `DateTimePicker` for one date-time instant, and `RelativeTime` for phrases such as “in 2 hours”.

const KeyboardHoverAndTouch = () => <Root delay={0}>(<>
      <Tooltip.Trigger appearance="outline">Focus or hover</Tooltip.Trigger>
      <Tooltip.Content>

      </Tooltip.Content>
    </>)</Root>;

direction-aware tooltip with preferred right placement

const Placements = () => <Root delay={0}>(<>
      <Tooltip.Trigger>Inline-end placement</Tooltip.Trigger>
      <Tooltip.Content placement="right">
        Placement follows React Aria direction and collision handling
      </Tooltip.Content>
    </>)</Root>;

      <Tooltip.Trigger>Persistent tooltip</Tooltip.Trigger>
      <Tooltip.Content>A controlled read-only tooltip</Tooltip.Content>
    </>)</Root>;

        <WarningIcon />
      </Tooltip.IconTrigger>

        This item requires attention

./src/primitives/Tooltip/Tooltip.mdx
Concise non-interactive descriptive help revealed from a button or named focusable icon by hover, focus, or touch.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Tooltip.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Concise non-interactive descriptive help revealed from a button or named focusable icon by hover, focus, or touch."
/>

# Tooltip

`Tooltip` gives an existing trigger concise, non-essential descriptive help without adding an action surface.

## Import

```tsx
import { Tooltip } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { WarningIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use it to clarify a control whose visible label cannot carry a short secondary explanation. Use persistent text for essential instructions, `Popover` for interactive content, and `VisuallyHidden` to supply an accessible label that does not need a visual popup. Tooltips never replace a control's accessible name.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Tooltip } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<Tooltip.Root>
  <Tooltip.Trigger appearance="outline">Export</Tooltip.Trigger>
  <Tooltip.Content>Download the current results</Tooltip.Content>
</Tooltip.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.KeyboardHoverAndTouch} />

## Anatomy

| Part                  | Purpose                                                               |
| --------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `Tooltip.Root`        | Coordinates delayed hover, focus, touch, and controlled open state.   |
| `Tooltip.Trigger`     | Full public `Button` used as an action described by the tooltip.      |
| `Tooltip.IconTrigger` | Compact focusable informational icon with a required accessible name. |
| `Tooltip.Content`     | Concise, non-interactive descriptive popup.                           |

Use exactly one trigger part. `IconTrigger.children` should be a decorative icon and `aria-label` names the icon itself. The tooltip adds description; it does not supply that name.

```tsx
<Tooltip.Root>
  <Tooltip.IconTrigger aria-label="Warning information" variant="danger">
    <WarningIcon aria-hidden="true" />
  </Tooltip.IconTrigger>
  <Tooltip.Content variant="danger">
    This item requires attention
  </Tooltip.Content>
</Tooltip.Root>
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SemanticIconTrigger} />

## State, timing, and presentation

Use `defaultOpen` for uncontrolled state, `open` with `onOpenChange` for controlled state, or `open` with `readOnly` for immutable state. `onOpenChange` receives the complete next Boolean. Opening delay defaults to `700`ms and closing delay to `0`ms; use shorter delays only where repeated inspection warrants it.

Content placement defaults to `top`; supported values are `top`, `bottom`, `left`, `right`, and top/bottom start/end. Collision handling may change the final position. `offset` defaults to `6` pixels. `variant` is `secondary` by default and may be `primary` or `danger`; keep icon and content variants aligned and never use colour as the only meaning.

Trigger inherits Button disabled and loading behaviour. A disabled native button cannot receive focus or hover consistently, so essential disabled explanations should remain visible. Tooltip itself has no invalid, empty, error, or loading state and Content must not contain interactive elements.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Native attributes and styling

IconTrigger and Content accept relevant `aria-*`, `data-*`, className, and element refs. Inline styling and conflicting interaction handlers are excluded.

## Keyboard, touch, internationalisation, and mistakes

Keyboard focus and pointer hover reveal Content after the delay; Escape dismisses it. Touch behaviour is provided by React Aria without making Content interactive. Keep content to a short phrase, translate it independently of stable application state, and allow wrapping. Start/end placement follows direction.

Do not put links, buttons, form controls, rich errors, or essential instructions in Content; do not use an unnamed IconTrigger; do not repeat the visible trigger label without adding information. Related components: `Popover`, `VisuallyHidden`, `IconButton`, and `Alert`.

./src/patterns/SectionHeader/SectionHeader.mdx
Compact content-section header with a fixed level-two heading, supporting description, and one directly related action.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './SectionHeader.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Compact content-section header with a fixed level-two heading, supporting description, and one directly related action."
/>

# SectionHeader

`SectionHeader` aligns a section's `h2`, concise supporting context, and one related action.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, SectionHeader } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use it at the start of a major region within a page when a visible boundary and one action clarify the section. Use `PageHeader` for the page's `h1` and page-level actions, `Card.Header` for content inside a card, or `Typography` when only a heading is needed.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, SectionHeader } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<section aria-labelledby="recent-updates-heading">
  <SectionHeader
    action={<Button appearance="ghost">View all updates</Button>}
    description="Latest changes across the workspace"
    id="recent-updates-heading"
    title="Recent updates"
  />
  {/* Section content */}
</section>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.WithAction} />

## Structure and composition

The pattern renders a semantic `header`, a fixed `h2`, an optional paragraph, and an optional action wrapper. `id` is assigned to the `h2`, not to the root header, so a surrounding region can reference it with `aria-labelledby`.

The action does not wrap and the title column shrinks. Prefer one concise low-emphasis control. Use `ButtonGroup` or a page-level action area when several controls are necessary, and test translated copy at compact widths.

## Accessibility, state, and internationalisation

Keep the document outline logical: because the title is always `h2`, use another composition when the region requires a deeper heading level. The visible title should identify the following region; provide a unique `id` when another element references it. The optional action must have its own accessible name and must make sense in relation to this section.

`SectionHeader` adds no keyboard interaction. The slotted action retains its own focus, disabled, loading, and activation semantics. The pattern has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, invalid, empty, loading, or error state; present content states below it, or use `StatePanel` when the entire region is replaced.

Translate `title`, `description`, and action copy. Logical flex alignment supports left-to-right and right-to-left direction without reversing application-owned content order.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use it as the page `h1`, assign its `id` expecting the root header to receive it, fit a toolbar into the single-action slot, or let action text obscure the heading at narrow widths. Related components: [PageHeader](?path=/docs/patterns-page-structure-pageheader--docs), [Card](?path=/docs/data-display-card--docs), [Typography](?path=/docs/foundation-typography--docs), and [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs).

 */

    aria-label="Display mode"

    onChange={fn()}
    options={options} />;

    aria-label="Visibility"

    options={[
      { label: 'Visible', value: 'visible' },
      { label: 'Hidden', value: 'hidden' },
    ]}
    orientation="vertical" />;
Docs doc ok
./src/patterns/SegmentedControl/SegmentedControl.mdx
Compact, labelled single-selection control for stable string options, with controlled, uncontrolled or read-only state, radio semantics and orientation-aware arrow-key navigation.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './SegmentedControl.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Compact, labelled single-selection control for stable string options, with controlled, uncontrolled or read-only state, radio semantics and orientation-aware arrow-key navigation."
/>

# SegmentedControl

`SegmentedControl` presents a short, mutually exclusive choice as adjacent selectable segments.

## Import

```tsx
import {
  SegmentedControl,
  type SegmentedControlOption,
} from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `SegmentedControl` for two or a few concise peer choices that immediately change a view or presentation mode. Every option is visible and selection is represented by a stable string value.

Use `RadioGroup` for a labelled form question, required answer, native form participation, descriptions or errors. Use `ToggleGroup` when you need multiple selection or compound child composition. Use `Tabs` when selection switches between labelled content panels, and `ButtonGroup` for momentary actions.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { SegmentedControl } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { useState } from 'react';

const displayOptions = [
  { label: 'List', value: 'list' },
  { label: 'Grid', value: 'grid' },
];

export function DisplayMode() {
  const [mode, setMode] = useState('list');

  return (
    <SegmentedControl
      aria-label="Display mode"
      onChange={setMode}
      options={displayOptions}
      value={mode}
    />
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Uncontrolled} />

## Anatomy and options

SegmentedControl is a fixed pattern rather than a public compound namespace. The root coordinates one selection and keyboard focus. Each entry in `options` becomes one adjacent toggle with radio semantics in single-selection mode.

```ts
interface SegmentedControlOption {
  disabled?: boolean;
  label: ReactNode;
  value: string;
}
```

Values must be unique, stable, locale-independent strings. Translate `label`, not `value`. `disabled` on an option prevents that option from being selected; root `disabled` prevents all choices from changing.

Keep the set short and labels concise. The component has no loading, invalid, error or dedicated empty presentation. Avoid rendering an empty `options` array; render an application fallback instead. If options are loaded asynchronously, show separate loading and error feedback before rendering the control.

## Selection state and callback semantics

- **Uncontrolled:** omit `value`; optional `defaultValue` selects the initial option. Optional `onChange` observes subsequent non-empty selections.
- **Controlled:** provide `value` and `onChange`. The callback receives the next selected option’s string value, never an array or DOM event.
- **Read-only:** provide `value` with `readOnly`; focus is preserved but selection cannot change.

The public value is a single string. An initial empty selection is possible when neither `defaultValue` nor `value` is supplied. The underlying single-selection group can represent no selection, but `onChange` only reports a selected string and does not report an empty array. Use controlled state or `RadioGroup` when the application must enforce and validate a required answer.

Controlled and uncontrolled props are mutually exclusive. Mutable controlled state requires `onChange`; SegmentedControl throws if JavaScript bypasses that TypeScript contract. Application state owns persistence, URL synchronisation, data loading and consequences of the selected mode.

## Orientation, keyboard and direction

`orientation` defaults to `'horizontal'`. Horizontal segments form one row; vertical segments form one column. Orientation controls both layout and arrow-key axis.

- Tab enters or leaves the group. Enter or Space selects the focused segment.
- Horizontal arrow keys move focus according to provider text direction. Vertical orientation uses Up and Down.
- Selected options expose radio-style checked state. The required `aria-label` names the group; visible option labels name the choices.
- Keep DOM option order logical and do not reverse the array for right-to-left content.
- Option labels are application-owned, translated copy and must tolerate expansion. Values are identifiers and are not localised.

SegmentedControl performs no routing and is not a tab-panel coordinator. It has fixed default ToggleButton appearance and size; there are no pattern props for appearance, variant, size, density or tone. `className` is for placement, not internal segment styling.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use translated labels as values, include many or long options, use segments for unrelated actions, duplicate values, or treat the control as navigation tabs. Use `RadioGroup` for a form answer, `ToggleGroup` for configurable selection, and `Tabs` for content panels.

Separator

layout-separator · ./src/primitives/Separator/Separator.stories.tsx
Separates related regions and exposes separator semantics unless marked as decorative.

 */

Decorative story ok
decorative divider hidden from assistive technology
Renders a horizontal visual divider that is explicitly removed from the accessibility tree because it adds no semantic relationship.
const Decorative = () => <Stack>
    <Typography as="span">Before</Typography>
    <Separator decorative orientation="horizontal" tone="default" />
    <Typography as="span">After</Typography>
</Stack>;
Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/Separator/Separator.mdx
A semantic or decorative divider with horizontal and vertical orientations.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Separator.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A semantic or decorative divider with horizontal and vertical orientations."
/>

# Separator

`Separator` draws a horizontal or vertical division and exposes separator semantics unless explicitly decorative.

## Import

```tsx
import { Separator } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use a semantic separator when the division helps users understand neighbouring regions. Use `decorative` when the same relationship is already clear from structure and the line is visual only. Prefer `gap` on `Stack` or `Inline` when you only need spacing, and use headings or separate landmarks for stronger structural boundaries.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Separator, Stack, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function Sections() {
  return (
    <Stack>
      <Typography>Current details</Typography>
      <Separator />
      <Typography>Previous details</Typography>
    </Stack>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Horizontal} />

## Orientation and semantics

Horizontal separators render an `hr`; vertical separators render a `div` with `role="separator"` and `aria-orientation="vertical"`. A vertical separator must be placed in a parent with a meaningful height. `decorative` changes the role to `presentation` and adds `aria-hidden="true"`.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and keyboard behaviour

Relevant native attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are accepted. `children`, `role`, `aria-orientation`, and inline `style` are intentionally component-owned or unavailable. There are no callback, controlled, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error states.

Meaningful separators are discoverable but not focusable and have no keyboard interaction. Do not add an accessible name unless it disambiguates a genuinely meaningful divider. Direction does not change the declared horizontal or vertical axis.

## Common mistakes

- Do not expose repetitive visual rules as semantic separators.
- Do not mark a meaningful structural division `decorative`.
- Do not use a vertical separator without height-bearing surrounding layout.

## Related components

Use `Stack` and `Inline` to place separators, `Surface` for a bounded region, and `Accordion` or `Disclosure` when the boundary also controls visibility.

/**

 */

        <Surface border="none" padding="md">

./src/primitives/Skeleton/Skeleton.mdx
Non-announcing visual placeholder that reserves loading layout with text, circle, or rectangle geometry.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Skeleton.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Non-announcing visual placeholder that reserves loading layout with text, circle, or rectangle geometry."
/>

# Skeleton

`Skeleton` reserves the shape of content while that content is loading without adding placeholder noise to the accessibility tree.

```tsx
import { Skeleton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use skeletons when the eventual layout is predictable and preventing layout shift helps orientation. Use [Spinner](?path=/docs/feedback-spinner--docs) for a compact activity mark, [ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs) for measurable work, or [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs) when the whole region has a meaningful loading or empty message.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Skeleton, Stack } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function LoadingSummary() {
  return (
    <section aria-busy="true" aria-label="Loading summary">
      <Stack gap="sm">
        <Skeleton className="w-2/3" />
        <Skeleton />
        <Skeleton className="min-h-32" shape="rectangle" />
      </Stack>
    </section>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Shapes} />

## Shapes, element, and tone

`shape` is `text` by default; `circle` provides a canonical avatar-sized placeholder and `rectangle` provides a minimum block height. Adjust width or height with `className` to mirror the real content. `as` defaults to `div`; use `as="span"` only when inline placement requires valid markup.

`tone` accepts `default`, `inverse`, or `danger` and defaults to `default`. Match tone to the host surface rather than using it as a data status. Motion is suppressed for reduced-motion preferences.

## Accessibility and state

Every skeleton has `aria-hidden="true"`, has no role, and accepts no children. Put `aria-busy="true"` and a translated accessible label or status on the owning content region where users need loading context. Skeleton has no callback, keyboard interaction, controlled value, disabled, invalid, empty, or error behaviour. Remove placeholders when real content or an error appears. Geometry and logical placement work in both directions.

`Skeleton` supports relevant attributes for its selected `div` or `span`, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `children`, `role`, and inline `style` are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not expose fake placeholder text to assistive technology.
- Do not use arbitrary shapes unrelated to the eventual layout.
- Do not leave a loading region permanently busy after an error.
- Do not use a skeleton as a disabled control or content-redaction treatment.

## Related components

[Spinner](?path=/docs/feedback-spinner--docs), [ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs), and [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs).

Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/SkipLink/SkipLink.mdx
A focus-revealed native fragment link that lets keyboard users bypass repeated page chrome.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './SkipLink.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A focus-revealed native fragment link that lets keyboard users bypass repeated page chrome."
/>

# SkipLink

`SkipLink` is the first keyboard destination on a page and moves users directly to its main content target.

## Import

```tsx
import { SkipLink } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use one near the beginning of every application shell with repeated navigation. It complements landmarks and headings; it is not a replacement for them. Use ordinary `Link` for visible navigation.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { SkipLink } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<>
  <SkipLink targetId="main-content">Skip to main content</SkipLink>
  <header>{/* Repeated navigation */}</header>
  <main id="main-content" tabIndex={-1}>
    Page content
  </main>
</>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.KeyboardFocus} />

## Behaviour

`targetId` is written without `#`; Breeze creates a native `#fragment` link. The matching target must exist and should be a meaningful landmark. Add `tabIndex={-1}` where the target is not naturally focusable so browsers can move focus consistently. The link is visually off-screen until focused and respects reduced-motion preferences.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Native attributes and states

Relevant native anchor attributes such as `aria-*`, `data-*`, and `hreflang` are supported. `href`, inline style, and click handlers are owned by Breeze. SkipLink has no disabled, loading, controlled, read-only, invalid, empty, or error state.

## Accessibility, internationalisation, and mistakes

Place it before all repeated focusable chrome. Tab reveals and focuses it; Enter follows the fragment. Use a concise translated action such as “Skip to main content”, keep the target id stable across locales, and test that focus lands visibly at the intended heading or region in both directions.

Do not use a missing or duplicated target id, put the link after navigation, suppress its focus styling, or route fragment activation through application navigation. Related components: `ApplicationShell`, `NavigationList`, `Link`, and `VisuallyHidden`.

    <Spinner label="Warning activity" size="md" variant="warning" />
    <Spinner label="Information activity" size="md" variant="info" />

Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/Spinner/Spinner.mdx
Compact indeterminate activity indicator that is either explicitly labelled or deliberately decorative.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Spinner.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Compact indeterminate activity indicator that is either explicitly labelled or deliberately decorative."
/>

# Spinner

`Spinner` indicates ongoing activity when the duration cannot be measured.

```tsx
import { Spinner } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use a spinner for compact indeterminate activity, especially inside an existing status layout. Use [ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs) when progress is measurable, [Skeleton](?path=/docs/feedback-skeleton--docs) to reserve a loading layout, and a disabled/loading-capable control pattern when activity belongs to one action.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { Inline, Spinner, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SavingStatus() {
  return (
    <Inline gap="sm" wrap={false}>
      <Spinner />
      <Typography as="span">Saving changes</Typography>
    </Inline>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Decorative} />

## Labelling

Omit `label` when adjacent visible text already communicates the activity. The spinner then has `aria-hidden="true"` and is purely decorative. Supply an application-owned translated `label` when it is the only status; Breeze renders `role="status"` and a visually hidden accessible name.

Do not supply both a spinner label and duplicate live status text. The animation stops under reduced-motion preferences while the status semantics remain.

## Sizes and variants

`size` accepts `sm`, `md`, or `lg` and defaults to `md`. `variant` accepts `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, or `dark` and defaults to `primary`. Select `light` only on a sufficiently dark surface and `dark` only on a light surface.

<Canvas of={Stories.SizesAndVariants} />

## State, keyboard, and direction

Spinner is indeterminate and read-only. It has no callback, keyboard interaction, disabled, invalid, empty, or error state. Render or remove it as application state changes. Its circular motion is direction-independent. Translate `label`; do not add directional copy to the decorative mark.

`Spinner` supports relevant native `span` attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `children`, `role`, and inline `style` are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not show an unlabelled standalone spinner with no visible status.
- Do not announce the same status twice.
- Do not use a spinner for measurable progress or as a permanent decoration.
- Do not rely on animation alone to communicate loading.

## Related components

[ProgressBar](?path=/docs/feedback-progressbar--docs), [Skeleton](?path=/docs/feedback-skeleton--docs), and [Alert](?path=/docs/feedback-alert--docs).

Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/Stack/Stack.mdx
A vertical layout with responsive, token-based spacing and bounded alignment.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Stack.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A vertical layout with responsive, token-based spacing and bounded alignment."
/>

# Stack

`Stack` arranges related children vertically while preserving their authored reading order.

## Import

```tsx
import { Stack } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Stack` for forms, page sections, cards, and other vertical flows whose spacing should use Breeze tokens. Use `Inline` for a horizontal flow, `Grid` for columns, or ordinary semantic HTML when no layout abstraction is needed.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, Stack, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function EmptyMessage() {
  return (
    <Stack align="start" gap="md">
      <Typography as="h2" level="heading-md">
        No saved views
      </Typography>
      <Typography>Create a view to reuse these filters.</Typography>
      <Button>Create view</Button>
    </Stack>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Default} />

## Layout and responsive spacing

`align` controls the horizontal cross-axis; `justify` controls distribution on the vertical main axis. `gap` accepts `none`, `xs`, `sm`, `control`, `compact`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl`, or `page`, either directly or as `{ base, sm?, md?, lg? }`. Breakpoints start at 681 px, 901 px, and 1181 px.

```tsx
<Stack gap={{ base: 'sm', md: 'xl' }}>
  <section>First</section>
  <section>Second</section>
</Stack>
```

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility, direction, and content

Relevant native `div` attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported; inline `style` is not. `Stack` has no selection, callback, loading, invalid, disabled, controlled, or read-only state.

`Stack` is a neutral `div` and adds no role, label, focus, or keyboard interaction. Use semantic children and preserve a logical DOM order. It remains vertical in both left-to-right and right-to-left layouts. Empty stacks render but usually indicate that conditional rendering belongs to the parent.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use `justify="between"` unless the stack has a meaningful available height.
- Do not use `align="center"` for long-form content that should remain easy to scan.
- Do not add margins to every child; set `gap` on the stack.

## Related components

Choose `Inline` for rows, `Grid` for two-dimensional layouts, `Center` for one centred composition, and `Separator` when a visible or semantic division is required.

Docs doc ok
./src/patterns/StatePanel/StatePanel.mdx
Complete contextual panel for an empty, error, success, warning, or informational region with guidance and optional recovery.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './StatePanel.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Complete contextual panel for an empty, error, success, warning, or informational region with guidance and optional recovery."
/>

# StatePanel

`StatePanel` replaces a substantial content region with a clear state, explanation, and optional next step.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, StatePanel } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { InfoIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use it for an empty view, failed region, completed setup, blocked state, or substantial informational state. Use `Alert` for inline feedback that should remain beside content, `Toast` for transient queued feedback, `Skeleton` or `Spinner` for pending work, and a field's `Error` part for validation.

The application owns state detection, retries, permissions, analytics, and domain copy. The pattern does not infer state from data or perform recovery.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, StatePanel } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { InfoIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

<StatePanel
  action={<Button onAction={() => createItem()}>Create item</Button>}
  description="Create the first item to begin using this screen."
  icon={<InfoIcon />}
  title="No items yet"
  variant="info"
/>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Empty} />

## Anatomy, variants, and state

The root is a `section` labelled by its generated `h2`. A large decorative `IconTile` sits beside the title and description; the optional action moves below the copy and stretches on compact screens. There are no public compound parts.

`variant` controls semantic marker colour and defaults to `info`:

| Variant   | Typical use                                            |
| --------- | ------------------------------------------------------ |
| `info`    | Neutral guidance or an empty state.                    |
| `primary` | A constructive product next step.                      |
| `success` | A completed outcome that deserves a full-region state. |
| `warning` | A condition requiring care before proceeding.          |
| `danger`  | A failure or blocked condition requiring recovery.     |

Colour does not set announcement behaviour or application status. Use explicit title and description copy. The pattern has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, disabled, loading, or invalid props. Disable or load the slotted action itself. For pending content, prefer progress feedback rather than describing loading as an error state.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalisation

`title` supplies the region's accessible name and must identify the actual state. The icon tile is decorative by default; do not rely on its shape, icon, or colour to communicate meaning. Keep guidance actionable and put one clear recovery or next-step control in `action`.

The panel is not a live region by default. For newly inserted urgent errors, choose `Alert` or deliberately add an appropriate native `role`/`aria-live` attribute only when immediate announcement is required; avoid repeated announcements during re-renders. The panel itself has no keyboard interaction, while the slotted action retains its own keyboard and callback semantics.

Translate all visible copy. Logical grid placement and alignment follow provider direction. Choose direction-neutral icons where possible; application-authored directional icons must account for right-to-left presentation.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use `danger` colour as the only error signal, place several competing actions in the slot, show an empty-state panel while data is merely pending, or assume insertion is announced. Related components: [Alert](?path=/docs/feedback-alert--docs), [Skeleton](?path=/docs/feedback-skeleton--docs), [Spinner](?path=/docs/feedback-spinner--docs), and [IconTile](?path=/docs/foundation-icontile--docs).

complete surface tone and border comparison

const TonesAndBorders = () => (
  <Grid columns={{ base: 1, md: 2 }}>
    <Surface tone="default">Default tone</Surface>
    <Surface tone="subtle">Subtle tone</Surface>
    <Surface tone="canvas">Canvas tone</Surface>
    <Surface tone="inverse">Inverse tone</Surface>
    <Surface border="none">No border</Surface>
    <Surface border="strong">Strong border</Surface>
  </Grid>
);

Renders a named native section when the contained application content forms a meaningful document region rather than a purely visual grouping.
const SemanticSection = () => <Surface
    border="default"
    padding="lg"
    tone="default"
    aria-label="Project summary"
    as="section">Summary content</Surface>;

./src/primitives/Surface/Surface.mdx
A canonical background, border, and responsive inset for grouped content.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Surface.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A canonical background, border, and responsive inset for grouped content."
/>

# Surface

`Surface` gives grouped content a canonical Breeze background, border treatment, and responsive inset.

## Import

```tsx
import { Surface } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Surface` as a low-level visual region when you need deliberate background, boundary, and padding choices.

## When not to use

Use `Card` when content has card anatomy and actions, or `Container` for page width and gutters. Avoid surfaces around every group: hierarchy should remain meaningful.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Stack, Surface, Typography } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function Summary() {
  return (
    <Surface as="section" aria-labelledby="summary-title" tone="subtle">
      <Stack gap="sm">
        <Typography id="summary-title" as="h2" level="heading-md">
          Summary
        </Typography>
        <Typography>Three items are ready.</Typography>
      </Stack>
    </Surface>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.TonesAndBorders} />

## Tone, border, and inset

`default` is the normal raised content surface, `subtle` creates quiet grouping, `canvas` matches the application canvas, and `inverse` is for content on the shell colour. Choose tone for hierarchy and contrast, not decoration. `border="strong"` increases boundary emphasis; `none` is appropriate only when surrounding contrast already defines the region.

`padding` accepts `none`, `xs`, `sm`, `control`, `compact`, `md`, `lg`, `xl`, `xxl`, or `page`, directly or as `{ base, sm?, md?, lg? }` at 681 px, 901 px, and 1181 px.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and semantics

Relevant native HTML attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported; inline `style` is excluded. There is no controlled, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error state.

Colour and borders add no accessible semantics. Choose `section` only for a labelled document section and `article` only for independently distributable content; otherwise retain `div`. Ensure custom content meets contrast requirements, particularly on `inverse`. `Surface` adds no keyboard or focus behaviour and works in both text directions.

## Common mistakes

- Do not select `section` or `article` solely for styling.
- Do not rely on tone alone to convey success, warning, or error state; use `Alert` where status semantics matter.
- Do not apply arbitrary background and padding classes when a public tone or spacing token expresses the design.

## Related components

Use `Card` for structured content, `Alert` for semantic feedback, `Container` for page bounds, and `Stack` or `Grid` for internal layout.

One keyed focusable tag item for use in a TagGroup collection, with stable identity, text representation, disabled state, and optional semantic action.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Tag.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="One keyed focusable tag item for use in a TagGroup collection, with stable identity, text representation, disabled state, and optional semantic action."
/>

# Tag

`Tag` is one keyed interactive item for a `TagGroup`; the containing group supplies selection and removal behaviour.

## Import

```tsx
import { Tag, TagGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `Tag` inside `TagGroup.List` for compact labels that may be focused, selected, acted on, or removed. Prefer `Badge` for passive status or metadata, `Button` for an action, and `ListBox.Item` for a conventional option collection. A standalone Tag story demonstrates the item, but production composition should provide TagGroup context and an accessible group label.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Tag, TagGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

<TagGroup.Root>
  <TagGroup.Label>Topics</TagGroup.Label>
  <TagGroup.List>
    <Tag id="accessibility" textValue="Accessibility">
      Accessibility
    </Tag>
    <Tag id="design" textValue="Design systems">
      Design systems
    </Tag>
  </TagGroup.List>
</TagGroup.Root>;
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Static} />

## Identity and behaviour

`id` is a required stable `string | number` used for focus, selection, actions, and removal. `textValue` is required plain text for typeahead and accessibility. Visible `children` may contain richer presentation, but must preserve the same meaning.

`disabled` prevents focus, selection, action, and removal. `onAction` receives this tag’s key when its semantic action is invoked. Selection and removal callbacks belong to `TagGroup.Root`; Tag has no controlled, uncontrolled, read-only, loading, invalid, or error state of its own.

## Keyboard and accessibility

Keyboard behaviour is coordinated by `TagGroup`: arrow keys move between tags, and selection, action, or removal keys depend on enabled group behaviours. Do not put links, buttons, or other interactive descendants inside `Tag`; use the tag’s semantic action or a separate control. Keep `id` independent of translated content and provide an accurate `textValue` for every locale.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes and related components

Do not use array indexes as IDs, omit `textValue`, nest interactive controls, or treat a Tag as a passive badge. Use `TagGroup` for collection state, `Badge` for status, `Button` for a command, and `ListBox` for a larger option collection.

 */

 */
variant?: VisualVariant = info

}

import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Toast.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Provider-scoped notification queue controlled by useToast, plus an explicitly controlled standalone Toast for specialised semantic ownership."
/>

# Toast

`Toast` provides transient, interactive notifications. Prefer the `useToast` controller for the queue owned by the nearest `BreezeProvider`; use standalone `Toast` only when the application must own placement and lifecycle itself.

```tsx
import { Toast, useToast } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use a toast for a brief update that does not need to remain in the page, such as confirmation after an action. Use [Alert](?path=/docs/feedback-alert--docs) for persistent inline feedback, field `Error` parts for validation, [AlertDialog](?path=/docs/primitives-overlays-alertdialog--docs) for a decision that blocks progress, and [StatePanel](?path=/docs/patterns-feedback-statepanel--docs) for a substantial page state.

Do not put critical instructions, long-form content, or the only recovery path in an auto-dismissing toast.

## Queued notifications

Call `useToast` beneath `BreezeProvider`. `show(options)` adds a provider-scoped notification and returns its string queue identifier; `dismiss(id)` closes that notification. The provider renders the primary labelled `ToastRegion` automatically and shows at most `toastLimit` notifications, which defaults to `3`.

```tsx
import { Button, useToast } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SaveButton() {
  const toast = useToast();

  return (
    <Button
      onAction={() => {
        toast.show({
          description: 'The revised record is now available.',
          lifetime: 5000,
          title: 'Changes saved',
          variant: 'success',
        });
      }}
    >
      Save changes
    </Button>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.ProviderQueueAndAction} />

## Content, actions, and lifecycle

`title` is required and supplies the notification's primary accessible name. `description` is optional supporting content. Both are application-owned `ReactNode` and must be translated.

`action` is `{ label: ReactNode, onAction: () => void }`. Activating it calls `onAction` once and dismisses the toast. Use one concise action; it must be safe after the initiating context changes. Every toast also has a Breeze-labelled close button.

`lifetime` is milliseconds: the default is `5000`, `null` persists until dismissal, and `0` dismisses immediately. Other values must be finite and non-negative or `show` throws `RangeError`. `onDismiss` runs once when a queued toast is dismissed, including action, close, timeout, immediate close, or controller dismissal, but not during provider teardown. Do not use it as the only persistence mechanism.

The queue identifier belongs to the current provider and should be treated as opaque. Controllers and identifiers must not be reused across provider boundaries.

## Standalone Toast

Standalone `Toast` is an explicitly controlled `role="alertdialog"` with polite, atomic announcement semantics. Supply `onDismiss` and remove it from application state when called. Its action calls `action.onAction` and then `onDismiss`. `closeLabel` defaults to the provider's translated close message.

```tsx
import { Toast } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ControlledNotification({ dismiss }: { dismiss: () => void }) {
  return (
    <Toast
      description="One live owner avoids duplicate announcements."
      onDismiss={dismiss}
      title="Controlled notification"
      variant="info"
    />
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.StandaloneSemanticOwner} />

## Variants and state

`variant` accepts `primary`, `secondary`, `success`, `danger`, `warning`, `info`, `light`, or `dark` and defaults to `info`. Choose by message meaning, not decoration; colour is supplemental to the title and description.

Toast has no loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error prop. Express those situations in translated content and choose a semantic variant. Do not disable dismissal while work is pending. Repeated updates should normally update application state or use a persistent status rather than flooding the queue.

<Canvas of={Stories.Variants} />

## Keyboard, accessibility, and direction

Queued toasts are placed in a labelled landmark region and exposed as interactive alert dialogs. React Aria's toast-region keyboard model lets keyboard users move to the region with F6, then use Tab and Shift+Tab among toast actions and close buttons; Escape dismisses the focused toast. Standalone `Toast` is focusable and its action and close controls use normal button keyboard activation. Never remove visible focus treatment or duplicate the same message in another live region.

Translate titles, descriptions, action labels, and any standalone `closeLabel`. Override the provider `messages.close` and `messages.notifications` for Breeze-owned queue labels. Logical inline placement follows the provider direction, and the provider-owned portal carries its locale and direction.

Standalone `Toast` supports relevant native `div` attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `children`, `role`, inline `style`, and the native `title` attribute are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not call `useToast` outside `BreezeProvider`.
- Do not render another primary `ToastRegion`; the provider already owns one.
- Do not set `lifetime={null}` for routine confirmations that should clear themselves.
- Do not place multiple competing actions or long interactive workflows in a toast.
- Do not retain a standalone toast after `onDismiss` fires.
- Do not duplicate one notification through both the queue and standalone `Toast`.

## Related components

[ToastRegion](?path=/docs/feedback-toastregion--docs), [Alert](?path=/docs/feedback-alert--docs), [AlertDialog](?path=/docs/primitives-overlays-alertdialog--docs), and [BreezeProvider](?path=/docs/foundation-breezeprovider--docs).

ToastRegion

 */

/**

Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/Toast/ToastRegion.mdx
Advanced additional presentation of the nearest BreezeProvider toast queue with a separately translated accessible label.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ToastRegion.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="Advanced additional presentation of the nearest BreezeProvider toast queue with a separately translated accessible label."
/>

# ToastRegion

`ToastRegion` renders an additional labelled presentation of the nearest `BreezeProvider` toast queue. The provider already renders the primary region automatically.

```tsx
import { ToastRegion } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Most applications should not render this component. Use it only when an advanced layout deliberately needs another presentation of the same provider-owned queue and the accessibility impact has been tested. Use [`useToast`](?path=/docs/feedback-toast--docs) to add and dismiss notifications; use standalone `Toast` when the application owns an independent notification source.

## Basic use

```tsx
import { ToastRegion } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function SecondaryNotificationRegion() {
  return <ToastRegion label="Secondary notifications" />;
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.AdditionalRegion} />

## Queue and lifecycle behaviour

The region reads the existing queue from the nearest provider; it does not create another controller, queue, limit, or notification lifecycle. `BreezeProvider toastLimit` still controls the maximum visible queued toasts. Rendering outside `BreezeProvider` throws an error.

Because an additional region presents the same queue, careless use can duplicate visible content, announcements, focus destinations, and actions. Do not add one merely to reposition the primary stack. Prefer configuring the provider portal host and application layout where possible.

## Accessibility, keyboard, and internationalization

`label` names the region and must distinguish it from the provider-owned primary region; when omitted it uses the provider's translated `messages.notifications` value. Translate an explicit label. Queued toasts retain their alert-dialog semantics, F6 region navigation, Tab and Shift+Tab action navigation, Escape dismissal, timeout behaviour, and close/action labels.

The region is fixed at the logical block start and inline end, so it follows the provider's left-to-right or right-to-left direction. The provider-owned portal carries `lang` and `dir`. Test focus movement and announcements with every region present.

ToastRegion has no controlled/uncontrolled value, callback, loading, disabled, invalid, empty, or error state. These belong to the shared queue and application notification content.

`ToastRegion` supports relevant native `div` attributes, including `className`, ARIA and data attributes, events, and a typed `ref`; `children`, `role`, and inline `style` are intentionally owned or excluded.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Common mistakes

- Do not render `ToastRegion` outside `BreezeProvider`.
- Do not render an additional region without a tested layout and accessibility reason.
- Do not give two regions the same accessible label.
- Do not expect a region to create an independent queue or change `toastLimit`.
- Do not use it as a replacement for `useToast`.

## Related components

[Toast and useToast](?path=/docs/feedback-toast--docs), [Alert](?path=/docs/feedback-alert--docs), and [BreezeProvider](?path=/docs/foundation-breezeprovider--docs).

ToggleButton

 */

 */

 */

 */

Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/ToggleButton/ToggleButton.mdx
A controlled, uncontrolled, or read-only two-state action.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ToggleButton.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A controlled, uncontrolled, or read-only two-state action."
/>

# ToggleButton

`ToggleButton` lets a user select or deselect one persistent two-state action.

## Import

```tsx
import { ToggleButton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `ToggleButton` for an action that remains on or off, such as showing a preview. Use `Button` for a momentary command, `Switch` for an immediately applied setting with a persistent label, `Checkbox` for form selection, or `ToggleGroup` for related valued toggles.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { ToggleButton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function PreviewToggle() {
  return <ToggleButton defaultSelected>Show preview</ToggleButton>;
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Uncontrolled} />

## State ownership

Use `defaultSelected` for uncontrolled initial state. For controlled mutable state, provide both `selected` and `onChange`; the callback receives the next boolean selected state. For intentionally immutable but focusable state, provide `selected` with `readOnly`. Do not mix `defaultSelected` with `selected`.

```tsx
import { useState } from 'react';
import { ToggleButton } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function PreviewToggle() {
  const [selected, setSelected] = useState(false);

  return (
    <ToggleButton selected={selected} onChange={setSelected}>
      Show preview
    </ToggleButton>
  );
}
```

`disabled` prevents focus and changes. `readOnly` preserves focus and activation semantics but keeps the controlled selection unchanged. The selected treatment is always solid; the configured `appearance` controls the unselected treatment. ToggleButton has no loading, invalid, error, or empty state.

## Composition with ToggleGroup

Inside `ToggleGroup`, give every `ToggleButton` a unique, stable string `value`. The group owns selection and keyboard coordination; do not also control each child’s `selected` state. Standalone toggles expose pressed-button semantics, while grouped toggles use selection semantics appropriate to the group mode.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility and keyboard behaviour

Relevant native button attributes such as `aria-*` and `data-*` are supported. Native `value`, `onChange`, pressed state, disabled state, class, and inline style are owned by the Breeze contract.

Use a stable label that describes the active state or feature rather than changing between contradictory verbs. Standalone `ToggleButton` exposes `aria-pressed`; `Enter` and `Space` request the next state. In a group, arrow-key behaviour comes from `ToggleGroup`. Icons accompanying text should be decorative. Labels must be translated and allowed to grow.

## Common mistakes

- Do not use a toggle for a one-off command.
- Do not provide `selected` without `onChange` unless `readOnly` is explicitly true.
- Do not change a controlled value in response to unrelated events without keeping the visible state consistent.
- Do not omit `value` inside `ToggleGroup`.

## Related components

Use `ToggleGroup` for related choices, `Switch` for a setting, `Checkbox` for form selection, and `Button` for momentary actions.

ToggleGroup

actions-togglegroup · ./src/primitives/ToggleGroup/ToggleGroup.stories.tsx
Coordinates single or multiple toggle selection and arrow-key navigation.

Component: src/primitives/ToggleGroup/ToggleGroup.tsx::ToggleGroup
Props:
/**
 * ToggleButton children with stable `value` props.
 */
children: ReactNode

/**
 * Initial selected values. Defaults to an empty selection.
 */

/**
 * Prevents all contained toggles from changing. Defaults to `false`.
 */
disabled?: boolean = false

/**
 * Allows more than one selected value. Defaults to `false`.
 */
multiple?: boolean = false

/**
 * Called with the next selected values.
 */

 * Layout and keyboard navigation axis. Defaults to `horizontal`.
 */

/**
 * Marks a controlled selection as intentionally immutable.
 */

/**
 * Ref to the rendered group.
 */

 * Current selected values.
 */

single-selection toggle group with stable values

const SingleSelection = () => <ToggleGroup aria-label="View mode" onSelectionChange={fn()}>(<>
      <ToggleButton value="grid">Grid</ToggleButton>
      <ToggleButton value="list">List</ToggleButton>
    </>)</ToggleGroup>;

      <ToggleButton value="italic">Italic</ToggleButton>

    </>)</ToggleGroup>;

  <Inline align="stretch" gap="xl" wrap={false}>
    <ControlledToggleGroupExample />
    <ToggleGroup
      aria-label="Read-only view mode"
      readOnly
      selection={['list']}
    >
      <ToggleButton value="grid">Grid</ToggleButton>
      <ToggleButton value="list">List</ToggleButton>

);

./src/primitives/ToggleGroup/ToggleGroup.mdx
A labelled group that coordinates single or multiple toggle selection and arrow navigation.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './ToggleGroup.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A labelled group that coordinates single or multiple toggle selection and arrow navigation."
/>

# ToggleGroup

`ToggleGroup` coordinates related valued `ToggleButton` children with single or multiple selection and orientation-aware keyboard navigation.

## Import

```tsx
import { ToggleButton, ToggleGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
```

## When to use

Use `ToggleGroup` for a compact set of closely related view or formatting choices. Use `SegmentedControl` for the opinionated pattern when its API fits, `RadioGroup` for a form question with one required answer, `CheckboxGroup` for a labelled form collection, or `Toolbar` for unrelated commands with no shared selection.

## Anatomy

`ToggleGroup` is the root selection and keyboard coordinator. Its direct interactive children are public `ToggleButton` components, each with a unique string `value`. There are no separate group label or item parts: label the root with `aria-label` or `aria-labelledby`, and put visible text or icons in each toggle.

```tsx
import { ToggleButton, ToggleGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';

export function ViewChoice() {
  return (
    <ToggleGroup aria-label="View mode" defaultSelection={['grid']}>
      <ToggleButton value="grid">Grid</ToggleButton>
      <ToggleButton value="list">List</ToggleButton>
    </ToggleGroup>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.SingleSelection} />

## Selection and callback semantics

Single selection is the default and reports zero or one selected value in a string array. Set `multiple` for independent values. `defaultSelection` sets uncontrolled initial state. For controlled mutable state, provide `selection` and `onSelectionChange`; the callback receives the complete next `string[]`, not only the changed item. For immutable focusable state, provide `selection` with `readOnly`.

```tsx
import { ToggleButton, ToggleGroup } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { useState } from 'react';

export function ControlledViewChoice() {
  const [selection, setSelection] = useState(['grid']);

  return (
    <ToggleGroup
      aria-label="View mode"
      selection={selection}
      onSelectionChange={setSelection}
    >
      <ToggleButton value="grid">Grid</ToggleButton>
      <ToggleButton value="list">List</ToggleButton>
    </ToggleGroup>
  );
}
```

`disabled` prevents all contained toggles from changing. Empty selection is valid unless the application enforces another rule; the component has no `required`, invalid, loading, or error presentation.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility, keyboard, and direction

Relevant native `div` attributes, notably `aria-label`, `aria-labelledby`, `id`, and `data-*`, are supported. Breeze owns selection changes, children, class, and inline style.

Always give the group an accessible label. Single-selection toggles expose radio-style checked state; multiple-selection toggles remain pressed buttons. `Tab` enters or leaves the group, `Enter` or `Space` selects the focused toggle, and arrow keys move focus on the configured axis. Horizontal navigation follows text direction; vertical navigation uses Up and Down. Keep DOM order logical and values locale-independent while translating visible labels.

## Common mistakes

- Do not omit the group label or child values.
- Do not reuse a value or derive values from translated labels.
- Do not mix controlled root selection with controlled child state.
- Do not use `multiple` when the choices are mutually exclusive.
- Do not use the group for unrelated commands; use `Toolbar`.

## Related components

Use `ToggleButton` alone for one state, `SegmentedControl` for the canonical pattern, `RadioGroup` or `CheckboxGroup` for form values, and `Toolbar` for command collections.

Toolbar

 */

/**

 */
ref?: Ref<HTMLDivElement>

Docs doc ok
./src/primitives/Toolbar/Toolbar.mdx
A labelled collection of related controls with orientation-aware arrow-key navigation.
import { ArgTypes, Canvas, Meta } from '@storybook/addon-docs/blocks';
import * as Stories from './Toolbar.stories';

<Meta
  of={Stories}
  summary="A labelled collection of related controls with orientation-aware arrow-key navigation."
/>

# Toolbar

`Toolbar` groups related interactive controls under toolbar semantics and coordinates arrow-key navigation.

## Import

```tsx
import { Button, IconButton, Toolbar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { DeleteIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';
```

## When to use

Use `Toolbar` for a compact collection of related commands, such as editor or item actions. Use `Inline` for layout without toolbar semantics, `ToggleGroup` for related selected values, `Menu` when actions should be collapsed, or `ButtonGroup` for a small canonical action arrangement.

## Basic example

```tsx
import { Button, IconButton, Toolbar } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui';
import { DeleteIcon } from '@motech-development/breeze-ui/icons';

export function ItemToolbar() {
  return (
    <Toolbar aria-label="Item actions">
      <Button>Edit details</Button>
      <IconButton aria-label="Delete item" variant="danger">
        <DeleteIcon />
      </IconButton>
    </Toolbar>
  );
}
```

<Canvas of={Stories.Horizontal} />

## Composition and orientation

The `Toolbar` root owns the toolbar role, label, layout, and focus coordination. Compose it from public interactive controls such as `Button`, `IconButton`, `ToggleButton`, or links. The root has no public compound parts. Use `horizontal` for ordinary action rows and `vertical` only when the visual arrangement and expected arrow axis are genuinely vertical.

Disabled state belongs to each child control; `Toolbar` has no group-wide disabled, loading, invalid, error, empty, selection, controlled, or read-only state. An empty toolbar provides no value and should not render.

## API

<ArgTypes />

## Accessibility, keyboard, and direction

Other relevant native `div` attributes such as `id`, `aria-*`, and `data-*` are supported. Children, class, and inline style are constrained by the public API.

Every toolbar needs an accessible name, especially when more than one exists on a page. `Tab` enters and leaves the toolbar; the arrow keys for its orientation move focus between enabled controls. Horizontal arrow behaviour follows the interface direction, while vertical toolbars use Up and Down. `Enter` or `Space` activates the focused button according to that child’s semantics. Each icon-only child still requires its own `aria-label`.

Keep related controls together, order them logically in the DOM, and translate both visible and accessible labels. Do not use toolbar semantics solely to obtain a flex row.

## Common mistakes

- Do not omit the toolbar’s accessible label.
- Do not place unrelated controls in one toolbar.
- Do not add manual arrow-key handlers or roving `tabIndex` values to children.
- Do not use `Inline` when users need toolbar navigation, or `Toolbar` when layout alone is required.
- Do not render an empty toolbar while actions are loading; render appropriate progress or omit the region.

## Related components

Use `Inline` for neutral layout, `ToggleGroup` for selection, `Menu` for collapsed actions, `ButtonGroup` for an opinionated action pattern, and `Separator` to divide meaningful command clusters where needed.

Typography

 * Semantic text colour. Defaults to `default`.

 * Adds canonical bottom rhythm to block elements. Defaults to `false`.
 */

 */

/**
 * Bounds text to a supported number of lines. Defaults to no clamp.
 */

/**
 * Ref to the rendered element.
 */

/**
 * Uses tabular numeral glyphs. Defaults to `false`.
 */
tabularNumbers?: boolean = false

/**
 * Truncates one line with an ellipsis. Defaults to `false`.
 */
truncate?: boolean = false

/**
 * Font weight override. Defaults to the selected level.
 */
weight?: "bold" | "medium" | "regular" | "semibold"
